About This Document
This document describes the basic functions and GUIs of NetEco.
Product Version
The following table lists the product version related to this document.
Product Name Product Version
NetEco V600R026C10
Intended Audience
This document is intended for the following engineers:
● Technical support engineers
● Maintenance engineers
Symbol Conventions
The symbols that may be found in this document are defined as follows.
Symbol Description
Indicates a hazard with a high level of risk which, if not
avoided, will result in death or serious injury.
Indicates a hazard with a medium level of risk which, if not
avoided, could result in death or serious injury.
Indicates a hazard with a low level of risk which, if not
avoided, could result in minor or moderate injury.

Symbol Description
Indicates a potentially hazardous situation which, if not
avoided, could result in equipment damage, data loss,
performance deterioration, or unanticipated results.
NOTICE is used to address practices not related to personal
injury.
Supplements the important information in the main text.
NOTE is used to address information not related to personal
injury, equipment damage, and environment deterioration.

contains all the changes made in earlier issues.

2
Getting Started
This section describes NetEco functions and home page, and how to log in to or
log out of the NetEco client.
2.1 Logging In to or Logging Out of the NetEco Client
This section describes the runtime environment requirements and how to log in to
or log out of the NetEco client.
2.1.1 Runtime Environment Requirements
To help you better operate the NetEco, the PC running the NetEco should meet
related requirements.
Table 2-1 lists the runtime environment requirements of the NetEco.
Table 2-1 Runtime environment requirements
Configuratio Basic Configuration Requirements
n Item
CPU I5-7500 or above
Memory 8 GB
Operating Windows 10 Professional 64-bit
system

Configuratio Basic Configuration Requirements
n Item
Browser Latest Chrome (Stable Channel) and Firefox (ESR Release) are
recommended.
NOTE
Operations on the browser may vary depending on browser versions but
are similar to the examples in the following steps. You are advised to
perform the operations based on actual situations.
● If you access the server through a proxy server, set the proxy
server as follows:
For Chrome:
1. Click in the upper-right corner of the browser and
select Settings.
2. On the Settings page, click Advanced, System, and Open
proxy settings in sequence.
3. In the Internet Properties dialog box, click LAN settings.
Select Use a proxy server for your LAN in the Proxy
server area, set Address and Port, and click OK.
4. Close the Settings tab page.
For Firefox:
1. Click in the upper-right corner of the browser and
select Options.
2. On the Options page, click Advanced, Network, and
Settings.
3. In the Connection Settings dialog box, select Manual
proxy configuration, set HTTP Proxy and Port, and click
OK.
4. Close the Options tab page.
NOTE
To ensure that the proxy server can access the server properly, check
that the network bandwidth meets the following requirements:
Uplink (from the browser to the server) data rate: ≥ 100 kbit/s;
downlink (from the server to the browser) data rate: ≥ 100 Mbit/s
● Set pop-up exceptions for the browser as follows:
For Chrome:
1. Click in the upper-right corner of the browser and
select Settings.
2. On the Settings page, click Advanced, Privacy and
security, Site settings, and Pop-ups and redirects in
sequence.
3. Click Add.
4. In the Add a site dialog box, enter https://server IP
address:server port number (https://10.10.10.1:31943
for example), and click Add.

Configuratio Basic Configuration Requirements
n Item
5. Close the Settings tab page.
For Firefox:
1. Click in the upper-right corner of the browser and
select Options.
2. On the Options tab page, click Content, Pop-ups, and
Exceptions in sequence.
3. In the Allowed Sites dialog box, set Address of website
to https://server IP address:server port number (https://
10.10.10.1:31943 for example), click Allow, and then click
Save Changes.
4. Close the Options tab page.
2.1.2 Logging In to the NetEco Client
The NetEco uses the browser/server (B/S) working mode. You can log in to the
NetEco using a web browser.
Prerequisites
User name and password are available.
Procedure
Step 1 In the browser address bar, enter https://IP address of the NetEco:31943, and
then press Enter.
NO TE
● You are advised to use the latest Chrome browser (Stable Channel version) or Firefox
browser (ESR version).
● The optimal resolution is 1920*1080.
● A certificate error message may be displayed on browser when you log in to the NetEco at
the first time. For details, see 9.14 How Do I Handle the Problem of Certificate Error or
Security Alarm Displayed in the Web Browser.
Step 2 Enter the User Name, Password, and click Login.

NO TE
● he preset user of NetEco is admin. For details about the password of user admin, see
the Default NetEco Web System User Information in NetEco Administrator Guide.
This user has all the operation rights on the managed objects. After the first login, you
need to change the password to ensure access security.
● If the number of online users reaches the maximum number supported by the system, a
message is displayed, indicating that you cannot log in. In this case, contact the system
administrator.
● After you enter the incorrect password for three consecutive times, you must enter the
verification code upon the fourth login. After you enter the incorrect password for five
consecutive times, the user account or the IP address is locked for 10 minutes.
----End
2.1.3 Logging Out of the NetEco Client
This section describes the operation of how to log out of the NetEco.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco client using a web browser. For details, see 2.1.2
Logging In to the NetEco Client.
Procedure
Step 1 Place the cursor on the in the upper right corner of the main menu of the
NetEco client, and click Log out.
When you log out of the NetEco, the web page returns to the NetEco login page.
----End

3
Device Management
Device management displays the layout and running status of devices on the
network in real time.
3.1 Device View
You can set views for the system to intuitively display equipment positions and
running status.
3.1.1 View
View management displays the layout and running status of devices on the
network, and monitors the running status of devices on the entire network in real
time.
3.1.1.1 Centralized Control View
In the centralized control view, you can manage devices in multiple domains in a
centralized manner.
Prerequisites
● Devices in different scenarios have been connected to the NetEco.
● You have the Browse View permissions.
● To set map parameters, you must have rights for Map Parameter Settings.
Context
View scenarios are classified based on the management domain type of the first-
level node under the root node. Table 1 lists the management domain type and
product type corresponding to each scenario.

types
Scenario Name Management Domain Product Type
Type
Site Site and room Equipment room power
management domain supply and smart site
Power Station Plant SUN2000
NO TE
The right panel of the centralized control view supports calculation of social contributions.
The formula is as follows:
● CO Reduction = Photovoltaic energy yield x CO emission reduction coefficient (0.475)
2 2
One kilowatt power is equivalent to 0.475 kg CO , and the emission reduction
2
coefficient is set to 0.475.
● Tree planting = CO emission reduction/Equivalent tree planting coefficient (18.3*40)
2
A tree absorbs 18.3 kg CO on average in one year, and each tree has an average
2
lifespan of 40 years. The equivalent tree planting coefficient is set to 18.3*40.
● Saving coal = Photovoltaic energy yield x Saving coefficient (0.4)
0.4 kg standard coal can convert to about 1 kilowatt power, and the saving coefficient is
set to 0.4.
Procedure
Step 1 Edit the scenario name.
1. Click in the upper left corner of the centralized control view page. The
Scenario-based Configuration dialog box is displayed.
2. Click in the Operation column to modify the scenario name.
3. Click OK.
Step 2 Set the map.
1. Click in the lower left corner of the page.
NO TE
– If the created management domain does not contain longitudes and latitudes and
the map is not used, the Settings button is not displayed. You need to click the
to display the icon.
– By default, the system uses an empty map. You can click to change the default
map.
2. In the displayed Map Parameter Settings dialog box, set related parameters
as required.

corresponding view.
----End
3.1.1.2 View
You can view the real-time site online map to learn about the running status of
sites on the entire network in real time and analyze and handle faulty sites.
Prerequisites
● If you want to display the GIS background map and weather information of
sites, ensure that you can access the Internet.
● You have the operation rights for Browse View.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > View.
The Overview tab page is displayed by default. You can view the overview
information about all sites on the entire network.
Step 2 Optional: Click in the lower corner of the page to switch the page skin.
Step 3 Click at the bottom left corner of the page to switch the background map to
the image set in the configuration module.
Step 4 Set page display parameters.
1. Click in the lower left corner of the page.
2. In the Parameter Settings dialog box, set related parameters as required.

3.1.1.3 Lithium Battery 2.0 Hibernation Parameters
This section describes the hibernation parameters that need to be set when you
modify device parameters on the View page.
Prerequisites
● One lithium battery 2.0 only or lithium batteries 2.0+2.0: All lithium batteries
must support the hibernation function. Lithium batteries 2.0+2.0 support
hibernation only when they have the same battery capacity and are charged
and discharged at the same time.
● The hibernation function is not supported in DC power system systems and
reused third-party power systems.
● Lithium batteries do not hibernate during battery tests.
Safety Conditions
The lithium battery hibernation function must be implemented while ensuring
power supply reliability. If the safety conditions for hibernation after full charge
and time-based discharge hibernation cannot be met, lithium batteries do not
hibernate.
● Hibernation after full charge
– The AC power supply is normal or the solar power supply is available.
– The battery current is greater than or equal to 0.
– All lithium batteries are fully charged.
● Time-based discharge hibernation
– PV deployment scenario: The AC power fails and the solar power supply
is not available. PV-only scenario: The solar power supply is not available.
– The battery current is less than or equal to 0.
– All batteries do not reach the DOD and do not meet the maximum
number of offline batteries.
Abnormal Conditions
Lithium batteries do not hibernate if any of the following exceptions occurs:
● System exception: The parallel system is being upgraded or the parallel
connection fails.
● DC output undervoltage or overvoltage occurs.
● An alarm related to lithium battery strings occurs: all lithium batteries
communication failure, battery fuse broken, battery disconnection, and
reverse battery connection. If any lithium battery string generates an alarm,
hibernation is not supported.
● An alarm related to lithium batteries: cell 1–24 fault, cell exception, input
reverse connection, abnormal shutdown, unlocking failure, busbar overvoltage

protection, charge high temperature, charge high temperature protection,
overcharge, overcharge protection, overdischarge, overdischarge protection,
charge overcurrent, charge overcurrent protection, heavy load warning,
discharge overcurrent protection, charge low temperature protection, and
discharge low temperature protection. If the number of lithium batteries is
greater than the minimum number of working lithium batteries (2 by default)
and the number of lithium batteries for which an alarm is generated is
greater than or equal to the maximum number of offline lithium batteries,
hibernation is not supported.
● When a battery is fully charged and hibernated, any hibernated lithium
battery self-discharges until the battery reports an undervoltage alarm. Then,
the battery exits hibernation and is charged.
If the preceding prerequisites and safety conditions are met and the preceding
exceptions do not occur, lithium batteries can be hibernated. Set Hibernation
Parameters as required.
Table 3-7 Hibernation parameters
Attrib Parameter Description
ute
Hibern Enable If Enable Hibernation is set to No, the hibernation
ation Hibernation function cannot be enabled. If Enable Hibernation
Param is set to Yes, the lithium battery hibernation
eters function is enabled only when the system meets
hibernation conditions.
Minimum Minimum number of lithium batteries that can be
Working Lithium discharged after the lithium battery hibernation
Battery Quantity conditions are met.
Minimum After the system meets the lithium battery
Discharge Rate hibernation conditions, if the discharge current of
the lithium batteries involved in discharging is
lower than the minimum discharge rate, the
lithium batteries need to be shut down.
Maximum After the system meets the lithium battery
Discharge Rate hibernation conditions, if the discharge current of
the lithium batteries involved in discharging is
greater than the minimum discharge rate, the
lithium batteries need to be started.
Rotation Period Interval for rotating lithium batteries
Hibernation Interval for the batteries to enter the hibernation
Stop Duration mode again after exiting the hibernation mode.
3.1.1.4 Plant Management

You can view the status of plants and devices.
Prerequisites
Devices have been connected to the management system and bound to a plant.
Context
Plants can be classified into the following five types based on their devices:
● PV plant: contains only PV devices and components, such as maximum power
point trackers (MPPTs) and inverters (non-PCS).
● Energy storage plant: contains only energy storage devices and components,
such as energy storage containers and cabinets.
● PV+storage plant: contains PV devices and energy storage devices.
● PV + storage charging station: contains PV devices, energy storage devices,
and charging piles.
● Charging-only station: contains only charging piles.
Viewing the Running Information of a Plant
1. Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose a target plant.
3. On the Overview page, you can view the basic plant information, energy
yield and revenue statistics, real-time status, and energy flow diagram.
– Basic plant information: displays the local weather forecast (current day
and the next two days), plant address, and string capacity.
NO TE
If the system does not display the weather of the place where the plant is
located, contact the system administrator to configure the weather service.
– Energy yield and revenue statistics: displays the energy yield and revenue
of the plant. The system calculates the sum of feed-in revenue of a PV
plant (Feed-in revenue = Feed-in electricity x Feed-in tariff) and savings
in electricity bills (Savings = Self-consumed electricity x Purchase price) to
display the benefits from the PV plant.
NO TE
● You need to set the electricity prices when adding a PV plant. Otherwise, the
system cannot calculate the revenue.
● If the price unit is inconsistent with the local type, contact the company
administrator to change the currency.
– Energy flow diagram: displays the current power supply direction of the
plant.
Before using the energy flow diagram to view the grid power, you need to
connect the power meter first. Otherwise, the power grid data is not

NO TE
● The energy flow diagram is displayed only when three or more elements
among PV, battery, grid, and load are involved in the system.
● You can click to view the energy flow map data in real time.
To change the enabling duration, contact Huawei technical support. By
default, this function is enabled for 1 minute. When this function is enabled,
the traffic consumption increases. The number of sharing times on the web
and app sides can be enabled for a maximum of 10 times per day.
– Energy management: displays the energy yield, energy consumption, and
self-consumption of a plant in different time dimensions, helping you
analyze the energy consumption trend and optimize electricity
consumption. In the energy storage scenarios, energy is stored and
discharged, improving the self-consumption rate.
– Environmental benefits: Unlike thermal power plants, PV power plants
generate electricity without CO2 emissions, which is equivalent to
planting trees.
– Alarm: displays the number of current alarms in the PV plant.
NO TE
If a large number of devices connected to the plant report data simultaneously, you may
need to wait for 30 to 40 minutes before the real-time data of all devices is reported to the
management system.
Viewing Charging Pile Running Information
1. Choose Monitoring > Monitoring from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose a charging-only plant.
3. On the Details page, you can view the basic information, charging status,
real-time data, and configuration parameters of the charging pile.
– Basic Information: displays the charging pile model, rated power, and
upper limit of the charging power.
– Charging state and energy charged: displays the charging status, charging
duration, and energy charged.
– Realtime Data: displays the rated power, accumulated charged

Charge Current, Switch Between Single and Three Phases, and
Working Mode.
3.1.1.4.2 Viewing and Managing Devices
You can monitor devices in real time. This helps you learn about the status of
devices in a timely manner and handle exceptions to ensure device safety.
Prerequisites
Devices have been connected to the management system and bound to a plant.
Context
Table 3-8 Device status description
Status Color Description
Running The device is running properly (including the on-grid,
off-grid, and terminal test status).
Standby The device is on standby or shut down unexpectedly or
on command.
Faulty The device is faulty or shut down unexpectedly.
Offline The communication is interrupted.
Loading The device has been identified and feature information is
being collected.
NO TE
● If the real-time device data cannot be obtained and the running information is
displayed as -, check whether the device is offline or faulty.
● The function screens vary with devices.
Navigation Path

2. In the navigation pane, select a company or plant, and click the Device
Management tab page.
You can manage and view all devices of the company or plant through Device
Management.
Viewing Device Information
On the Device Management page, you can view the communication status and

information. The ESS supports the 3D view.
● Overview: Displays the physical and logical relationships, status, and real-
time data of devices and their subordinate devices in a topology view. You can
click a device icon to view the running information of subordinate devices.
● Details: You can view running parameters such as the device status, real-time
device data, basic information, and smart switch details.
NO TE
If there are strings connected to the inverter, you need to set string parameters when
creating a PV plant. Otherwise, the string details are blank. Historical Information:
Supports query of the device status in a specified period.
NO TE
●
If the data in a certain period is incomplete or lost, you can re-collect the lost data.
Viewing ESS Information
The Smart String Energy Storage System (ESS, commonly in cabinets or
containers) has a complex internal structure. The 3D view displays the actual
layout and working status of each component inside the system, improving O&M
efficiency. In the navigation pane, select the ESS and its components, including the
energy storage unit (ESU), energy storage rack (ESR), energy storage module
(ESM), and heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC). Click
to enter the 3D view.
NO TE
o ensure the smoothness and accuracy of 3D views, the PC client must meet certain
requirements.
Running status and real-time data: Click a component in the navigation pane or click a
component in the 3D view to view the actual layout, operating status, and real-time data of
the component.
● Details: Click to view detailed information about each
component.
● Configuration: Click to set device parameters.
● Historical data: Click to query the operating status of
each component within a specified period.
● Warning: Click to track and handle the real-time active alarms
and historical alarms of components.
● 2D: Click to switch to the 2D view. In the 2D view, click
to switch to the 3D view.

device list and click Set Parameters. Alternatively, click a device name and set
device parameters on the Configuration page.
NO TE
● The parameters that can be set vary according to the device model. For details
about how to set the parameters, see the user manual of the device.
How to obtain: Visit https://support.huawei.com/enterprise/en/category/fusion-
solar-pid-1600073963553?submodel=doc and enter your device model to search
for the corresponding user manual.
● If a user changes the Authentication Password of a device in the management
system and delivers the password to the device, the device fails to reconnect to the
management system after being deleted. In this case, you need to reset the
password on the device.
● If a device is connected to another management system from one management
system, delete the device from the original management system and then connect
the device to the new management system.
● Deleting a device: Select one or more devices from the device list and click
Delete.
● Changing a device name:
– Modifying a Device Name
In the device list, click in the Operation column.
– Modifying Device Names in Batches

file.

exported file based on the site requirements.
iv. Click Batch Rename.

upload it.
vi. Click Execute.
The execution result is displayed in the lower part.

click View.
3. Click Replace.
NO TE
● To replace an optimizer, go to the Details page.
● When an inverter is replaced, the new inverter can inherit the total energy yield
data from the old inverter. After the old inverter is replaced, the data is
automatically calibrated on the new inverter. If automatic calibration fails due to
device disconnection or poor network quality, manually calibrate the device after
the fault is rectified.
Exporting Device Information
● Exporting basic information: Click Export Basic Info, or select one or more
devices and click Export Basic Info to export basic information about all or
selected devices.
● Exporting performance data: Select one or more devices and click Export
Performance Data.
Task Management
Click in the lower right corner of the page to view and process historical tasks
for exporting basic information, exporting performance data, and setting
parameters.
3.1.1.4.3 Viewing and Managing Plant Layouts
After the optimizer is connected to the management system, you can browse the
plant view to obtain the PV module status and energy yield.
The plant layouts include physical and logical layouts.
● Physical layout: Displays the actual installation positions of modules to help
you quickly locate a faulty optimizer.
● Logical layout: Displays the logical relationship between the inverters and the
PV modules mounted with optimizers to help you check the connections

NO TE
After optimizers are connected to the management system, the logical layout is displayed
by default. You can create a physical layout based on the actual installation positions of the
modules. For details, see Automatically Generating a Physical Layout and Manually
Creating a Physical Layout.
Figure 3-1 Physical layout
Figure 3-2 Logical layout
Automatically Generating a Physical Layout
Context
A large-sized PV plant contains a large number of PV modules. If there is only one
physical layout, it is difficult to find or locate PV modules. The system can classify
PV modules by area using the Tab Management function. Specifically, users can
manage PV modules of different areas (for example, on various rooftops) in the
same plant by tab page, ensuring efficient optimizer search and locating.
Before creating a physical layout, you are advised to plan tabs based on the PV

You have correctly pasted the SN labels of the optimizers to a physical layout
template, taken a photo of the template, and saved the photo.
Creating a Physical Layout
1. Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose a plant and click Layout.
NO TE
● By default, the Physical Layout tab has been created. If a large number of
optimizers need to be managed, you can add more physical layout tabs to
distribute the optimizers deployed on different rooftops among multiple physical
layouts. For details, see Tab Management.
● A maximum of 10 tab pages can be added to a physical layout, and each tab page
supports a maximum of 2400 PV modules.
3. Select a physical layout and click Edit. The Physical Layout Configuration
page is displayed.
4. Click Manage Drawings, click Upload, and upload a physical layout template
to the system.
NO TE
You can view and manage the uploaded drawings on the Manage Drawings tab page.
For details, see Drawing Management.

5. Click Identify Drawings, select the drawing to be added to the physical
layout, click Identify, and complete drawing identification as prompted.
NO TE
For the first identification, set Layout position, Optimizer-to-module ratio, and
Layout based on the actual optimizer installation. This setting will be used for
subsequent drawing identification. To change the settings, choose Device List >
Optimizer-to-module ratio or choose Identify Drawings > Set.

6. Optional: If there are multiple drawings, select and identify them one by one,
and drag the new PV modules to adjust their positions.
NO TE
If a PV module exists on the current tab page, the system adds the new PV module
layout to the lower part of the current view by default. To change the position, click
Set, select Layout position based on site requirements, and click Identify to add the
new PV module to the canvas.
7. Click Save in the upper right corner to save the physical layout.
NO TE
● If some QR codes cannot be identified, manually bind optimizers by referring to Binding
optimizers.
● After a physical layout is generated, you can view the physical location, status, and
energy yield of each optimizer. For details, see Viewing a Physical Layout.
Tab Management

● Adding a tab: Click and add a tab as prompted.
● Viewing a tab: Click and select a desired tab.
● Changing a tab name: Click , select a desired tab, click , and
change the tab name as prompted.
● Deleting a tab: Click to select a desired tab, click , and delete the
tab as prompted.
Drawing Management
Click the tab and click . In the Manage Drawings area, view and manage
drawings.
● Uploading drawings: Click Upload and upload the drawing as prompted.
● Viewing drawings: Double-click a drawing to view its content.
● Deleting a drawing: Select a drawing and click Delete to delete the drawing
as prompted.

Prerequisites
The layout diagram of the optimizer installation positions has been prepared.
Creating a Physical Layout
1. Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, select a PV plant, and click the Layout tab.
NO TE
By default, the Physical Layout tab has been created. If a large number of optimizers
need to be managed, you can add more physical layout tabs to distribute the
optimizers deployed on different rooftops among multiple physical layouts. For details
about tab management, see Tab Management.
3. Select a physical layout and click Edit. The Physical Layout Configuration
page is displayed.
4. Drag diagram elements to the canvas on the right by referring to the layout
drawing of the actual positions of inverters and PV modules, and set diagram
element installation parameters based on site requirements.
5. Select a diagram element to move it based on the actual position of a
module.
6. Binding an inverter: In the Device List area, drag an inverter to the inverter
diagram element for binding.
7. Binding optimizers: In the Device List area, set Optimizer-to-module ratio
based on site requirements and drag the optimizers to the positions of the
optimizer diagram elements for binding. When multiple modules share an
optimizer (that is, 1+N), drag the optimizer to the first diagram element and
click other modules to be bound to the optimizer in sequence.
8. Click Save in the upper right corner to save the physical layout.
Tab Management
On the Physical Layout tab page, manage the tabs.
● Adding a tab: Click and add a tab as prompted.
● Viewing a tab: Click and select a desired tab.

● Changing a tab name: Click , select a desired tab, click , and
change the tab name as prompted.
● Deleting a tab: Click to select a desired tab, click , and delete the
tab as prompted.
Viewing a Physical Layout
Procedure
1. Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, select a PV plant, and click the Layout tab.
3. On the Physical Layout tab, click to select a layout and view related
information.
– Figure 3-3 shows the screen description.
Figure 3-3 Screen description
– Optimizer status
Icon
Status Normal Faulty Offline Disconnecte
d
– PV module color
The color of a PV module indicates the ratio of the optimizer output
power to the rated optimizer power. The following figure shows the
mapping.
– Viewing details: Move the mouse pointer to an optimizer to view
information such as the energy yield, output power, and output voltage.
– Viewing the energy yield: Click next to Yield in the upper right corner
to view the energy yield today, this month, and this year, and the total
energy.
– Zooming in, zooming out, or restoring a layout: Click and in the

layout and scroll the mouse wheel to zoom in or out. Click to restore
the layout.
– Viewing the connection between inverters and PV modules: Click in
the lower right corner. The PV modules connected to the same inverter
are rendered in the same color.
– Clicking in the upper left corner to display the device tree. Select an
optimizer from the device tree and view its position in the layout.
Optimizer Disconnection Detection
Detects optimizer disconnections, detects faulty optimizers in a timely manner,
and rectifies faults to reduce energy yield loss of PV modules.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation tree on the left, select a PV plant, and click the Layout tab page.
If no physical layout diagram is created for the plant, the Logical Layout page is
displayed.
Step 3 Click Disconnection detection in the upper right corner of the page.
● If there are multiple inverters in the plant and all of them are equipped with
optimizers, select the inverter to be detected in the displayed dialog box and
click Confirm.
● If only one inverter in the plant is equipped with an optimizer, the detection
task is directly executed after you click Disconnection detection.
Step 4 If a disconnected optimizer is detected, you can quickly locate the optimizer based
on the physical layout diagram and rectify the fault based on the repair
suggestions.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
After the disconnection fault is rectified, perform the disconnection detection
again to ensure that the fault is rectified.

3.1.1.4.7 Viewing EMMA Revenue and Energy Forecast
The AI-powered energy management assistant (EMMA) provides intelligent
energy scheduling and management functions. Based on big data analysis, it
accurately predicts the power generation and consumption, and intelligently
stores, purchases, and sells electricity to achieve optimal system performance and
maximize financial benefits.
Prerequisites
The owner has enabled the EMMA function for the plant.
Viewing EMMA Revenue and Energy Forecast
1. Choose Device Management > Device View > View from the main menu.
2. In the navigation tree on the left, select a PV plant for which EMMA has been
enabled and click the EMMA tab to view the revenue information, revenue
comparison, and energy forecast and analysis.
– Viewing revenue information: You can view the number of days when the

can view the comparison between the revenues when EMMA is enabled
and disabled.
– Viewing energy analysis: In the Energy Forecast area, you can view
details about the energy yield, power consumption, and battery charge
and discharge in the past 24 hours, and energy forecast in the next 24
hours.

3.1.2.2 Battery Overview
On the Overview page, you can view the usage of batteries on the entire
network, and analyze and handle faulty batteries in a timely manner.

A site with battery devices installed has been configured, and the site is properly
connected to the NetEco.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Battery Analysis area, click Battery Quantity Analysis. The Battery
Overview page is displayed.
Task Operation
Querying battery 1. Set the battery type to Battery, set search criteria,
information and click Query.
2. If the battery type to Battery, you can perform the
following operations.
– Click a site name in the site column to go to the
site details page of Real-Time Monitoring and
view site details.
– Click a battery name in the Battery column to
view the basic information, health status, battery
status, and historical information of the battery.
– Click a Battery Group name in the Battery
Group column to view the basic information,
remaining capacity, battery group status, and
historical information of the battery group.
Querying battery 1. Set the battery type to Battery Group, set search
group information criteria, and click Query.
NOTE 2. If the battery type to Battery Group, you can
When the model or perform the following operations.
installation date of a
lithium battery string or – Click a site name in the site column to go to the
lead-acid battery string site details page of Real-Time Monitoring and
is changed, the total view site details.
discharge capacity of the
– Click a Battery Group name in the Battery
battery string will return
zero. Group column to view battery group
information.
– Click a risk level in the Risk Level column go to
the battery details page of Real-Time
Monitoring and view battery group details.
Exporting data Based on service requirements, click Export to export
the page information to the local PC for data viewing.
----End

This section describes how to view battery alarms that need to be focused and
processed by setting filter condition.
Prerequisites
During initial configuration, a site with battery devices installed has been
configured, and the site is properly connected to the NetEco.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Intelligent Security area, click Alarm. The Battery Alarm page is displayed.
● Monitoring alarms
Table 3-10 Alarm monitoring methods
Task Task Description
Monitoring alarms On the Alarm page, monitor the alarms reported by
using the alarm list all NEs and systems in real time.
Monitoring alarms The alarm indicators in the upper right corner of the
using alarm Alarm page show the number of critical alarms,
indicators number of major alarms, number of minor alarms,
and number of warning alarms.

Task Task Description
Monitoring alarms
Click in the upper right corner of the Alarm page
using the statistics
to view the alarm statistics charts. The statistical
panel
result is obtained based on the filtered alarms.
The Top 10 Alarms, Duration, Top 10 Alarm
Sources, Severity, and Status statistical charts can be
displayed on the statistics panel. Four statistical
charts can be displayed at the same time. You can
click in the upper right corner of the statistics
panel and then select the chart to be displayed from
the drop-down menu in the upper right corner of
each chart.
On the statistics panel, you can click any statistical
result to quickly display the alarms that meet the
condition. If you close the statistics panel, the filter
criteria selected on the panel are automatically
deselected.
NOTE
– Alarm management allows a maximum of 10 statistics
panels to be opened by online users concurrently.
– You can click in the upper right corner of the
statistics panel to manually refresh the statistical data.
Within 10 minutes after you open the statistics panel or
manually refresh the statistical data, the system
automatically refreshes the data every minute.
● Querying alarms
a. You can click Template Management in the upper left corner of the
page to view all filter templates and select a filter template from the
Quick Filter panel. Users with the Administrators role can manage
templates saved by all users.
The following types of templates are provided:
▪
Favorite: You can add the templates that you often use to your
favorites.
▪
Custom: Filter templates customized by the current user, which can
be shared to other users. You can set a custom template as the key
template. The alarms filtered according to the key template are
displayed in both the emergency maintenance notification and alarm
list.
▪
Shared: Available filter templates shared by other users.
▪
Default: Default filter template.
▪
Other: Filter templates that are not shared by other users. These
templates are visible only to users with the Administrators role.

NO TE
The filter templates on the Current Alarms, Alarm Logs, Historical Alarms,
Masked Alarms, Event Logs, Event Log Statistics, and Alarm Log Statistics
pages are independent from each other and cannot be shared.
b. If the filter templates on the Template Management panel do not meet
your requirements, click Filter in the upper left corner of the Current
Alarms page. Set filter criteria and click OK to search for the alarms to be
concerned about and handled.
c. Click Save or Save As to save the current filter criteria as a filter
template. Not only the filter criteria, but also the alarm display column
information and the combo sorting information configured by users are
saved in the filter template.
d. Export current alarms. Alarms can be exported to an .xlsx or .csv file.
When the exported file format is set to .xlsx and the number of alarms
exceeds 100,000, the file is exported as a .zip package. When the
exported file format is set to .csv and the number of alarms exceeds
10,000, the file is exported as a .zip package.
▪
Export some alarms: Select the alarms to be exported, click Export,
and choose Selected.
▪
Export all alarms: Click Export and choose All.
NO TE
Only the information about the alarm columns displayed on the page can be
exported. You can click in the upper right corner of the alarm list to set the
alarm columns to be displayed.
● Parameter Description
For details about parameters on the alarm filter panel, see Table 3-11.
Table 3-11 Description of parameters on the alarm filter panel
Parameter Description
Alarm name Name of the alarms to be queried. You can import or
export the selected alarms or export all alarms when
selecting alarms.
Alarm source Device where an alarm is generated.
If multiple alarm source conditions are set, the filtering
result is the union of all the conditions. That is, all the
alarms that meet any of the conditions are displayed on
the page.
Severity Alarm severity, including critical, major, minor, and
warning. By default, all alarm severities are selected. You
must select at least one alarm severity.

Parameter Description
Alarm status Alarm status, including the acknowledgment and
clearance status, maintenance status, and validity status.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
For Historical Alarms, this parameter does not include
the clearance acknowledgment status. For Event Logs, this
parameter does not include the clearance
acknowledgment status and validity status.
Last occurred Time when the alarm last occurred.
This parameter is displayed as Occurred in Alarm Logs,
Masked Alarms, and Historical Alarms, indicating the
last occurrence time of an alarm.
Cleared Time when an alarm is cleared.
You can set this parameter in the Advanced settings area
on the Alarm Logs or Historical Alarms page. This
parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms page.

Parameter Description
Advanced You can set criteria to filter desired alarms.
settings
If advanced criteria are set, the filtering result is
determined by the intersection of all the criteria. That is,
only the alarms that meet all the advanced criteria are
displayed.
Operator description (case-sensitive, except ends with):
– contains: Filters the alarms that contain the specified
character string.
– does not contain: Filters alarms that do not contain
the specified character string.
– begins with: Filters alarms that begin with the
specified character string.
– ends with: Filters alarms that end with the specified
character string.
– is in: Filters the alarms that are the same as the
specified character string. For example, in the Location
Info area, set Operator to is in and set Value to XXX.
In this case, the location information of the filtered
alarms contains XXX, and the alarms whose location
information contains XXX123 are not displayed.
– is not in: Filters alarms that are different from the
specified character string. For example, in the Location
Info area, set Operator to is not in and set Value to
XXX. In this case, the location information of the
filtered alarms does not contain XXX, and the alarms
whose location information contains XXX123 are
displayed.
– is blank: Filters the alarms if their values of this
parameter are empty.
– is not blank: Filters the alarms if their values of this
parameter are not empty.
To improve the maintenance efficiency, you are advised to
select Root alarms, Aggregated alarms, and Normal
alarms for Associated alarm ID. This parameter is not
displayed on the Masked Alarms or Historical Alarms
page. Meanings of the options:
– Normal alarms: Indicates that the alarms do not have
root alarms or correlative alarms and aggregated
alarms or original alarms.
– Root alarms: Alarms that cause multiple correlative
alarms.
– Correlative alarms: Alarms that are generated due to
the root alarms.
– Aggregated alarms: After alarms that meet the
aggregation rules are reported, new alarms are

Parameter Description
generated, which corresponds to multiple original
alarms.
– Original alarms: Alarms that meet the aggregation
rules correspond to an aggregated alarm.
For details about parameters in the alarm list, see Table 3-12.
Table 3-12 Description of parameters in the alarm list
Name Description
Alarm Serial Alarm SN. When an alarm is generated, it may be
Number modified multiple times. For example, its severity may
change or it may be cleared. Each SN corresponds to one
alarm.
Alarm Matched An alarm meets the rule and is handled according to the
Rule Name rule settings.
Occurrences Number of occurrences or merged times for an alarm of
an alarm source, that is, the repeated or merged alarms
have the same location information and alarm ID. This
parameter helps users to increase concerns on the alarm.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
or Historical Alarms page.
Alarm Source Device where an alarm is generated.

Name Description
Name Name of an alarm. An alarm name indicates what faults
occur on an alarm source. For example, the high CPU
usage alarm can be known by its name that the alarm is
sent from the CPU.
The meanings of the icons displayed next to alarm
names are as follows:
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is a root
alarm. You can expand the row that contains the
alarm in the list to view its correlative alarms.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is a
correlative alarm. You can expand the row contains
the alarm in the list to view its root alarms.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is an
aggregated alarm.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is an original
alarm.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is an invalid
alarm.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is a
maintenance alarm.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm is a toggling
alarm. You can set Toggling Identifier in the
Advanced settings area to filter alarms.
– : The icon is displayed when an alarm has remarks.
Click the alarm name to view the remarks on the
alarm details page.
Alarm ID ID of an alarm, which corresponds to the alarm name.
Severity Four alarm severities: critical, major, minor, and warning.
Acknowledgmen Acknowledgment status of the alarm. Available options
t Status are:
– Acknowledged
– Unacknowledged
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Clearance Status Clearance status of an alarm. Available options are:
– Cleared
– Uncleared
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.

Name Description
Toggling Times Number of times that alarms that meet the toggling
conditions are reported during the toggling.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Possible Causes Possible causes of an alarm, which can provide guidance
for alarm handling.
Maintenance Maintenance status of an alarm. For example, the
Status NORMAL state means that the alarm source is running
normally instead of being commissioned or upgraded.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Acknowledged Time when an alarm is acknowledged.
On
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Cleared Time when an alarm is cleared.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Source Serial Identifier allocated by alarm management to the source
Number system.
Alarm Source Type of the device that generates an alarm.
Type
Type Type of an alarm, indicating the type of the cause which
triggers the alarm.
Operation Operations that users can perform on an alarm.
– Acknowledge
– Clear
– Check
– Send email notifications
– Send SMS message notifications
– Set masking rules
– Set identification rules
– Set notification rules
– Set severity and type redefinition rules
– Set name redefinition rules
– Set intermittent/toggling rules
– Set aggregation rules
– Switch the view

Name Description
Location Info Information that assists in fault locating. Based on the
information, you can quickly locate the location where
an alarm is generated.
List Serial Serial number of an alarm in the current alarm list.
Number
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
or Historical Alarms page.
First Occurred If the alarm is a merged alarm, its first occurrence time
is displayed. If the alarm is not a merged alarm, its
generation time is displayed.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
or Historical Alarms page.
Arrived On Time when the alarm arrives at alarm management.
Last Occurred If the alarm is a merged alarm, its last occurrence time is
displayed. If the alarm is not a merged alarm, its
generation time is displayed.
On the Masked Alarms and Historical Alarms pages,
this parameter is displayed as Occurred.
Acknowledged User who acknowledges the alarm. If the alarm is
By unacknowledged, the user who unacknowledges the
alarm is displayed in the Acknowledged By column.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Cleared By User who clears the alarm.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Handler Username of the current alarm handler. The O&M
personnel can assign alarms to handlers.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Auto Clear Whether an alarm can be automatically cleared.
– Yes: The alarm can be automatically cleared. After the
fault that triggers the alarm is rectified, Clearance
Status of the alarm automatically is changed to
Cleared.
– No: The alarm cannot be automatically cleared. After
the fault that triggers the alarm is rectified, you must
manually clear the alarm.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Other Information irrelevant to alarm locating to help users
Information understand the alarm.

Name Description
Remarks Alarm comments entered by users.
Setting method: Select an alarm and click Comment to
enter comments.
Reason ID ID of the specific cause of the alarm.
Clearance Type Alarm clearance type:
– (Blank): uncleared.
– normal clear: A device fault is rectified and alarm
management receives a clear alarm. Then the alarm
is automatically cleared.
– restore clear: After a device restarts, alarm
management detects whether a fault exists. The
original fault alarm is automatically cleared.
– manual clear: An alarm is manually cleared.
– configure clear: A resource object is deleted, and the
alarms generated by the resource object are
automatically cleared.
– correlation clear: When receiving a root alarm of
uncleared correlative alarms, alarm management
handles the alarms using a correlation rule, reports
the root alarm, and automatically clears the
correlative alarms. The clearance type of the
correlative alarms is correlation clearance.
– clear from system: The system clears earlier alarms
according to the full-cache processing rule because
the storage space is limited.
– status switch clear: Because the device status
switches, the active alarm in the previous status is
automatically cleared and is reported again in the
device status after the switch. The clearance type of
the active alarm in the previous status is status switch
clearance.
– alarm synchronization-based clear: During alarm
synchronization, if some uncleared alarms exist in
alarm management but cannot be found in the
alarms synchronized from devices, these uncleared
alarms are automatically cleared.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Validity The default value is Valid. You can set it to Invalid on
the Identification Rules page.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.

Name Description
Duration Duration from Last Occurred to Cleared On.
This parameter is not displayed on the Masked Alarms
page.
Resource ID ID of the alarm source.
Device Category Alarm source categories.
Threshold Rule Threshold for reporting an alarm, that is, the threshold
of the fault-related measurement indicator. For example,
the High Ambient Temperature alarm is reported when
the ambient temperature exceeds the threshold.
Threshold When a threshold-crossing alarm is reported, the alarm
Information generation value is displayed in the threshold
information. When the threshold-crossing alarm is
cleared, the alarm generation and clearance values are
displayed in the threshold information.
NOTE
The clearance value is displayed in the threshold information
only when the threshold-crossing alarm is cleared.

Quick O&M personnel can quickly set In the Operation column that

for the quick entries. Only to select a rule to be set.
setting authorized users can perform

rules about the rules, see Table
3-15.

Rule Description
Send Email The information about the selected alarms is
Notification immediately sent to O&M personnel by email.
NOTE
To send notifications to relevant personnel, you need to enter
their personal information, such as mobile numbers and
email addresses. You are obligated to take considerable
measures, in compliance with the laws of the countries
concerned and the user privacy policies of your company, to
ensure that the user's personal information is fully protected.
Send SMS The information about the selected alarm is
Message immediately sent to the O&M personnel by SMS
Notification message.
NOTE
To send notifications to relevant personnel, you need to enter
their personal information, such as mobile numbers and
email addresses. You are obligated to take considerable
measures, in compliance with the laws of the countries
concerned and the user privacy policies of your company, to
ensure that the user's personal information is fully protected.
Set Masking Rule You can set an alarm masking rule to mask alarms
that you are not concerned about. The masked alarms
will not be displayed on the Current Alarms page.
Set Identification After an identification rule is set, the system
Rule automatically sets a status identifier for the alarms
that match the rule. When monitoring or querying
alarms, you can set filter criteria to filter out the
alarms. For example, you can set the alarms generated
in commissioning scenarios to Maintenance status.
Set Notification The system automatically sends the information about
Rule alarms that meet the rule conditions to O&M
personnel based on the notification method and
content template set in the rule. In this way, the O&M
personnel can learn about the alarm information and
take corresponding measures in a timely manner.
Setting After a rule is set, intermittent or toggling alarms can
Intermittent/ be discarded or masked to reduce interference caused
Toggling Rule by repetitive alarms.
Set Aggregation After an aggregation rule is set, the system
Rule automatically aggregates the repeated alarms
reported within the specified period into one alarm.

Button Description
Auto Refresh – Enable: Alarms on the current page are refreshed in
real time. By default, alarms are refreshed every 3
seconds.
– Disable: Locks the alarms on the current page. In this
case, only the alarm information on the current page
is updated. If other alarms are reported, the alarms
are not displayed on the page.
Quick Filter Displays selected alarms quickly.
Refresh interval Changes the automatic refresh interval.
Refresh interval Changes the automatic refresh interval. When an alarm
is reported to the NetEco, the NetEco obtains the alarm
reporting frequency.
Combo Sorting Sorts alarms by multiple alarm fields. A maximum of
four alarm fields can be set.
Clear When the fault that triggers an alarm is rectified but the
alarm is not cleared automatically, click Clear to
manually clear the alarm. After the alarm is cleared, its
status is changed to Cleared.
Acknowledge/ After an alarm is acknowledged, the alarm will be or has
Unacknowledge been handled. When the alarm is acknowledged, the
alarm status is changed from unacknowledged to
acknowledged.
If you want another engineer to handle the alarm, you
can unacknowledge the alarm. When the alarm is
unacknowledged, the alarm status is changed from
acknowledged to unacknowledged.
Specify Handler Specifies the user who handles the alarm. The specified
handler is displayed in the Handler column. The options
are as follows: Myself, Other Users, and Cancel.
Comment Edits the comments of an alarm.
Export Alarms can be exported to an .xlsx or .csv file. When the
exported file format is set to .xlsx and the number of
alarms exceeds 100,000, the file is exported as a .zip
package. When the exported file format is set to .csv and
the number of alarms exceeds 10,000, the file is
exported as a .zip package.
A maximum of five users can export some alarms at a
time, and a maximum of two users can export all alarms
at a time.

Button Description
Set as Sets selected alarms as invalid alarms or valid alarms.
Invalid/Set as When monitoring or querying alarms, you can set filter
Valid criteria to filter out such alarms, improving alarm
handling efficiency. The O&M personnel can set alarms
that they are not concerned about as invalid alarms.
Set as Under Sets selected alarms to the maintenance or normal state.
Maintenance/Se When monitoring or querying alarms, you can set filter
t as Normal criteria to filter out such alarms, improving alarm
handling efficiency. For example, you can set the alarms
generated in commissioning scenarios to Maintenance
status.
Send Email Immediately sends information about the selected
Notification alarms to O&M personnel by email.
NOTE
To send notifications to relevant personnel, you need to enter
their personal information, such as mobile numbers and email
addresses. You are obligated to take considerable measures, in
compliance with the laws of the countries concerned and the
user privacy policies of your company, to ensure that the user's
personal information is fully protected.
Send SMS Immediately sends the information about the selected
Message alarm to the O&M personnel by SMS message.
Notification
NOTE
To send notifications to relevant personnel, you need to enter
their personal information, such as mobile numbers and email
addresses. You are obligated to take considerable measures, in
compliance with the laws of the countries concerned and the
user privacy policies of your company, to ensure that the user's
personal information is fully protected.

3.1.2.5 Battery Test
Users can select a proper test mode based on the power supply conditions and
load status. Users can test the site batteries on NetEco to check the battery status.
Prerequisites
● You have the operation rights for Battery Management and Test Control.
● The site is properly connected to the NetEco and no major or critical alarm is
reported by site batteries.
Context
● Site batteries include Huawei batteries and third-party batteries. Huawei
battery tests are classified into short tests and standard tests. For details
about third-party batteries, see the corresponding manufacturer information.
– Short test: Perform a periodic short test to activate batteries and to
estimate their instant discharge performance. Standard test: Perform a
battery discharge test in a long period to evaluate the battery status
accurately and comprehensively.
– After a battery test is started, you can view real-time voltage, current,
temperature, and remaining electricity of the battery in the battery
management window. The site reports the preceding data on a 5-minute
basis.
● If you cancel the subscription to any of the battery voltage, current,
temperature, and remaining capacity during the battery test, the site stops
reporting the real-time data of the corresponding indicator and the result is

test, the site will change the battery before executing the discharge test.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Battery Analysis area, click Battery Test.
Task Operation
Setting battery
parameters 1. Click in the Operation column and set
battery test parameters. For details about Huawei
battery parameters, see Table 3-17. For details
about third-party battery parameters, see the
corresponding manufacturer information.
2. (Optional) Apply battery parameters in batches.
a. In the Set Test Parameters dialog box, click
Batch Setting.
b. Select the desired sites.
3. Click OK.
Starting a battery test ● Starting tests in batches
1. Click Batch Test.
2. In the displayed dialog box, select a test type
and set Maximum number of sites per round
and Interval between two rounds of tests.
NOTE
If there is a large amount of site data, you are
advised to perform tests in batches to reduce the
risk of site shutdown.
3. Select Ignore the latest tests.
The sites that have been tested within a
specified period are ignored.
4. Select sites.
5. (Optional) Click Generate Test Plan to view
and customize the number of battery test
rounds.
6. Click Start Test.
● Starting a single test
1. Click in the row where the battery string is
located and select a test type.
2. In the dialog box that is displayed, set Task
Name and click Confirm to start the battery
test.

Task Operation
Viewing a battery test
● View the latest battery test report. Click in
report
the Operation column. For details about Huawei
battery parameters, see Table 3-18. For details
about third-party battery parameters, see the
corresponding manufacturer information.
● To view historical battery test reports, click to
expand the navigation pane, click Battery
Reports, and select the corresponding report.
Viewing test start failure Click Test Startup Failure Records to view the
records failure records of starting Battery Test and the
failure cause of each record.
Querying batch test Click Batch Test Records to view the records of the
records tasks for which Battery Test is successfully started
and the battery test reports.

and export the latest battery test report.

reports synchronize the existing historical test reports on the

batteries.

Mode
● Group Discharge: The discharge test affects
batteries. Therefore, you are advised to use this
function when the service volume is low (for
example, after 00:00) or the mains supply is
stable.
● Alternate Discharge: Only one lithium battery
group discharges at a time. Except the lithium
batteries that are tested during the discharge, the
remaining lithium batteries supply power to
loads. This prevents site breakdown caused by
lithium batteries that cannot supply power loads
due to mains outage after the lithium batteries
discharge.

conditions must be met:
– The monitoring device type is SMU02X or SMU06T2
(only 01076957 is supported). The software version
must be iSitePower-ICT V100R024C10 or later.
– The monitoring device type is SCC800. The software
version must be SCC810 V100R025C10 or later.
– In the lithium battery scenario, intelligent lithium
batteries are used.

Current Limit the discharge test mode is set to Alternate
Coefficient Discharge.
Enable AC Indicates whether a battery test is required when
Outage Test the mains supply fails and batteries are used to
supply power.
Autom / A battery test mode initiated by the monitoring
atic software. If the battery test fails to be started, the
Test system records the failed task.

Parameter Description
Result Battery test results, which are categorized as follows:
● Test normal: When the preset conditions (time
and capacity) are met, the test ends normally.
● Ended abnormally: The test is stopped in other
cases (such as manual operation, power fault
recovery, and temperature).
● Battery alarm: The battery voltage is below the
preset test end voltage.

(V)

Voltage (V)

● 100%: The test is complete.
● N/A: The test is not complete.

Discharge (A)
Total Discharge Ah Cumulative battery discharge capacity in a test.
(AH)

categorized as follows: Under Voltage, Over the test
time limit, Automatic outage test will be interrupted
if the mains power fails, End the test manually, and
Termination of the fault alarm.

reports on batteries, including the Battery Running, Battery Charge/Discharge

the reports in line charts to learn about the running status of batteries.

You are authorized to use the Battery Management function.
Procedure

Step 2 In the Battery Report module, click Battery Test History Report .
Step 3 In the displayed Battery Test History Report window, select the required sites, set
the time range, and click Query.
Battery test reports meeting the preceding query criteria are displayed in the right
pane.
NO TE
When querying historical battery test data of the APM site, only the battery test data after
the APM site is connected to the NetEco is provided.
Step 4 Compare battery discharge curves shown in Figure 3-4 and learn battery
performance changes.
NO TE
● Battery discharge curves for short-time battery tests cannot be compared.
● When you compare battery discharge curves, select an early discharge curve for
reference. If a battery discharge curve differs significantly from the standard one, the
battery may be faulty or deteriorating. If this occurs, repair the battery or replace it with
a new one.

Step 5 Click to view the details about a battery test report. For details about the
related parameters, see Table 3-18.

3.1.2.6.2 Viewing Detailed Information About Bad Batteries
This section describes how to view the line chart of cell voltage to identify bad
cells in batteries.
Prerequisites
● You are authorized to use the Battery Management function.
● Cell detectors have been configured at sites, and the data of performance
counters have been reported.
● PowerCube 1000 V300R002C03 or a later version is used on site.
Context
● A ESU Monitor can monitor a maximum of 24 cells.
● An ESMU can monitor a maximum of 16 cells.
● The battery detector measures and reports cell voltage every five minutes.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.

search criteria, Click Query.
Step 4 View the battery information curve.
NO TE
● By default, only the line chart of cell voltage for the first cell is displayed in the right
pane. You can click the legend for a certain cell under the line chart to display the
corresponding line chart or not. A maximum of five line charts can be displayed in the
right pane.
● The line chart provides cell voltage data at intervals of 5 minutes and can be displayed
on multiple pages. The horizontal coordinate represents time in hours with one hour as
a regular interval.
----End

3.1.2.7 Battery SOH
This section describes how to check battery state of health (SOH). Before
maintaining batteries at a site on schedule, you can check the SOH of batteries on
the NetEco and establish the corresponding maintenance policy based on the
check result.
Prerequisites
● You are authorized to use the Battery management function.
● PowerCube 1000 V300R002C03 or a later version is used on site.
● The Lithium battery mgmt package(per site) license has been activated for
the NE to which batteries belong.
Context
SOH refers to the ratio of the current battery capacity to the initial battery
capacity, which can be categorized as follows:
● Healthy: greater than 70%.
● Subhealthy: greater than 50% and less than 70%.
Abnormal: less than 50%.
● No Data(Device):
– the reporting of related counters is not supported by an NE.
– the counter value reported by an NE is invalid.
– the reporting of related counters is supported by an NE, but the counters
have not been reported, or errors occur during counter aggregation.

Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Battery Analysis area, click Current Battery Health Analysis.
Step 3 View the SOH of batteries at the site, as shown in following figure:
● Figure 3-5 shows how to view the SOH of batteries at the site for the
PowerCube version.
Figure 3-5 View the SOH of batteries at the site

3.1.2.11 Discharge Quota
The NetEco supports license control over the power discharge of the hybrid power
system to enable batteries to work circularly. This saves fuel consumption and
reduces customers'; investment costs. Currently, only lithium batteries are
supported.
Prerequisites
A site with batteries has been configured and connected to the NetEco.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Discharge Quota module, click .
Operation Tasks Operation Method
Setting discharge quota 1. Click Setting.
2. In the License Policy Setting dialog
box, set the discharge quota.
3. After the setting is complete, click
OK.
Exporting discharge quota Click Export to save the site data on
the current page to the local PC.

Operation Tasks Operation Method
Refreshing discharge quota Click Refresh to refresh site data on
the current page.
----End
3.1.2.12 Risk Analysis Details
NetEco analyzes device alarms, real-time data, configuration information, and
performance data to quickly identify device risks, providing a basis for device
reconstruction and update. In addition, it provides warning information and risk
clearance suggestions for customers.
Prerequisite
A site with batteries has been configured and connected to the NetEco.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the Battery Analysis area, click Battery Risk Analysis.
Step 3 Select a site domain from the Select Region drop-down list and click Query.
If You Need To... Then...
Exporting risk analysis Click Export to save the statistics on the current page
data to the local PC.
Viewing historical data Click History Details, select the query time, and
query historical risk analysis data.
NOTE
The system queries data on the current day by default and
allows you to export historical data.
Viewing risk data
Click to go to the battery group page and view
details
the component information.
Viewing data by region Click Region or Factory.
or factory
● Statistics by Region: collects statistics on the
number of battery groups by risk level.
● Statistics by Factory: collects statistics on the
number of battery groups by vendor and whether
the battery groups have risks.
● Statistics by Risk Category: collects statistics on
the number of risks generated by battery strings,
the number of times each risk type occurs, and the
proportion of each risk type.

The NetEco can be used to remotely control the self staggering, self boost, self
hybrid, and staggering hybrid use of lithium batteries, and summarize and analyze
the working status of lithium batteries. Currently, only lithium batteries are
supported.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 In the IoT Gateway Battery Configuration area, click .
Step 3 On the IoT Gateway Battery Configuration page, click in the Operation
column of the site.
Step 4 In the Parameter Setting dialog box, set the intelligent power consumption mode
and parameters.
In intelligent power consumption mode, the functions are mutually exclusive.

3.1.2.14 Customizing Filter Condition for Battery Status
This section describes how to save frequently used filter criteria for battery alarms
as user-defined filter condition. Users can directly use them to query battery
status.
Context
The system provides one filter criterion by default: ALL.
NO TE
You cannot delete or modify the default filter condition.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose Device Management > Device View > Battery Management.
Step 2 On the Battery KPI page, click any link to go to the corresponding feature page
and click on the left.
Step 3 In the navigation tree on the left, click Status.
Step 4 Select Set Filter Condition from the Filter Condition drop-down list box on the
