8.2.2.3 Modifying Personal Client IP Address Policies
A client IP address policy provides a control mechanism for checking the
accessibility of the IP address used by an external access request. In this way, users
can log in to the NetEco only from specified IP address segments. With the
Update ACL Policy permission, you can configure your personal client IP address
policies. ACL is short for access control list.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Settings > Personal Settings from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Personal Client IP Address Policies.
Step 3 On the Personal Client IP Address Policies page, create, view, or modify your
client IP address policies.
NO TE
The client IP address policies you created or modified take effect for the user you are
currently logged in as.
----End

After the home page information is configured, the page set by the user is
displayed by default upon the next login, which helps users quickly view the
menus that they want to follow.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Personal Settings.
Step 2 In the navigation pane on the left, choose Home Settings.
Step 3 Paste the copied page URL to the URL bar on the home page and click Apply.
----End
8.2.3 Log Management
By managing fault logs of devices, users can learn the running status of devices.
8.2.3.1 Querying Logs
A log records the operations and major events of the management system. By
querying logs, you can learn about the running status and operation details of the
management system.
Prerequisites
You have the operation rights for Log Management.
Context
Management system logs are classified into security logs, system logs, and
operation logs.
● Security logs record security operations that are performed on the
management system, such as logging in to the server, changing passwords,
creating users, and logging out of the server.
● System logs record the events occurred on the management system, such as
abnormal running, network failures, and attacks to the management system.
These logs help you analyze the NMS status and rectify faults.
● Operation logs record the user operations that are performed on the
management system.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Log Management.
Step 2 In the navigation tree in the left pane, choose Security Logs, System Logs or
Operation Logs.
Step 3 On the page, view the log information, or set search criteria to view specified log

details.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
You can export the logs and back them up for future analysis.
NO TICE
Switching between pages will cause log exporting to be interrupted.
If... Then...
Export all logs 1. Click Export All to export all logs.
2. In the File Download dialog box, click Save.
3. Select a save path and click Save.
Export the selected logs 1. In the log list, select the check box before
Event Name of the required log.
2. Click Export Selected to export the selected
logs.
3. In the File Download dialog box, click Save.
4. Select a save path and click Save.
8.2.4 Log Forwarding Settings
The log forwarding settings feature can forward security logs, operation logs,
system logs, and other types of logs to Syslog servers — servers that can receive
logs through the Syslog protocol. The forwarded logs will be permanently saved
on Syslog servers so that users can query, trace, and analyze them to identify
security risks, diagnose issues, and rectify faults.
8.2.4.1 Overview of Log Forwarding Settings
This section describes basic information about the log forwarding settings feature,
including its definition, benefits, scenarios, functions, and principles.
8.2.4.1.1 Definition
The log forwarding settings feature converts security logs, operation logs, system
logs, and other types of logs to Syslog packets and forwards them to Syslog
servers.

The log forwarding settings feature enables centralized management of logs.
Users can query, trace, and analyze logs on Syslog servers to identify security risks,
diagnose issues, and rectify faults.
● For security logs, operation logs, and system logs, this feature ensures log
traceability if logs are deleted from both databases and hard disks due to log
dump and insufficient space.
● For other types of logs, this feature enables users to centrally query and
analyze them on Syslog servers.
8.2.4.1.3 Scenarios
The log forwarding settings feature is used for querying and analyzing security
logs, operation logs, system logs, and other types of logs on Syslog servers in real
time. It helps users centrally manage logs and promptly identify and eliminate
security risks. It ensures log traceability if logs are deleted from both databases
and hard disks due to log dump and insufficient space.
8.2.4.1.4 Functions
On the GUI, users can configure the Syslog servers to which logs will be
automatically forwarded.
8.2.4.1.5 Standards Compliance
This section describes the standards and protocols that the log forwarding settings
feature complies with.
Table 8-30 lists the standards and protocols that the log forwarding settings
feature complies with.
Table 8-30 Standards and protocols
Stan Description
dard/
Proto
col
RFC RFC 3164 defines the packet format used when syslog messages are
3164 forwarded over User Datagram Protocol (UDP).
RFC RFC 3195 defines the packet format. RFC 3195 describes two methods
3195 for transferring syslog messages over Transmission Control Protocol
(TCP), providing reference for reliable transfer of logs.
RFC RFC 5424 defines the packet format used when syslog messages are
5424 forwarded over TLS.
NOTE
For security purposes, the system uses TLSv1.3 by default.

This section describes the prerequisites for deploying and operating the log
forwarding settings function. Based on the prerequisites, you can evaluate whether
this function can be used and make necessary preparations, such as checking
whether a license is required and obtaining hardware requirements.
License Requirements
The log forwarding settings feature is not license-controlled.
Compatible Product Versions
The log forwarding settings feature does not require interaction with peripheral
products.
8.2.4.1.7 Principles
The log forwarding settings feature allows users to configure syslog servers on the
GUI so that logs will be converted to syslog packets and forwarded to the syslog
servers.
Figure 8-14 shows the principles of log forwarding settings.
Figure 8-14 Principles of log forwarding settings
The log forwarding settings feature converts logs in the log management
database and log forwarding database into syslog packets and forwards them to
the syslog server.
● If UDP is used, the system forwards syslog packets only to the active syslog
server. Therefore, no standby syslog server is required in this case.
● If TLS or TCP is used, the system attempts to connect to the active syslog
server in each period. If the connection fails, it attempts to connect to the

forwarded only to the active syslog server.
– If the system fails to connect to the active syslog server, it attempts to
connect to a standby syslog server. If the standby syslog server is
successfully connected, the system forwards logs only to that server and
reports the alarm "Alarm of the Switchover to the Standby Syslog
Server".
– If the standby syslog server cannot be connected either, the system will
not forward logs but it reports the alarm "Alarm of the Failure to Connect
the Master and Standby Syslog Servers".
NO TE
● To ensure successful communication between the system and syslog servers, clear the
alarms in a timely manner.
● For security purposes, the system uses TLSv1.3 by default.
● For details about the syslog packet structure, see "Log Forwarding Packets" in
Administrator Guide.
8.2.4.2 Configuring Log Forwarding Servers
The system will delete logs if there are more than 1000 log files or the log files
occupy more than 1024 MB disk space or have been stored for more than 45 days.
To trace and manage the logs for timely risk identification, issue diagnosis, and
fault rectification, you can configure log forwarding servers to forward logs.
Prerequisites
● You have obtained the trust certificate of the syslog server, if Transfer
protocol is set to TLS.
● You have the Log Transfer Management permission.
● You have obtained the IP addresses, port numbers, transfer protocols, and
syslog levels of the active and standby syslog servers to be interconnected.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Log Forwarding Setting from the
main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Forwarding Server.
Step 3 On the Forwarding Server page, click Create.
Step 4 Set syslog server parameters. Figure 8-15 shows the page for configuring log
forwarding servers. For details about some of the parameters, see Table 8-31.

Table 8-31 Syslog server parameters
Parameter Description
Active server The port numbers of the active and standby servers must
port/Standby be in the range of 1 to 65535.
server port

Parameter Description
Transfer protocol TLS, UDP, and TCP are supported.
● UDP cannot ensure transmission reliability. Connection
Status in the forwarding server list determines whether
logs can be successfully sent out as syslog packets. It
does not mean that packets can successfully arrive at
syslog servers.
● TLS is recommended, because it is more secure than
UDP and TCP. If TLS is selected, the system encrypts logs
before forwarding them to a syslog server.
● If TCP or TLS is selected, the log forwarding service sends
syslog packets to the active server first. If the sending
fails, the log forwarding service sends syslog packets to
the standby server. After the active server is restored, the
log forwarding service sends logs to the active server
again. If UDP is selected, syslog packets are sent only to
the active server. If TLS is selected, import the trust
certificate of the syslog server as follows:
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Management
from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Service Certificate
Management.
3. On the Services page, click SyslogService.
4. Click the Trust Certificates tab.
5. Click Import and configure certificate parameters.
NOTE
For security purposes, the system uses TLSv1.3 by default.
Syslog protocol The header of the forwarded log packet vary with syslog
protocol. The following uses Timestamp (log operation
time) as an example:
● RFC 5424: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX, for example,
2020-03-10T17:49:33.000+09:00
● RFC 3164: MM dd HH:mm:ss, for example, Aug 9
10:47:47
Syslog level When setting Syslog level, consider the levels of all logs to
be forwarded.
Required string If Required string is set, only the logs that contain the
specified strings will be forwarded. Regular expressions and
wildcard characters are not allowed in Required string.
Syslog server time You can set Syslog server time zone based on the time
zone zone where the syslog server is located.
After the setting, the time in the forwarded syslog packets
is automatically converted to the time of the time zone
where the syslog server is located.

Parameter Description
Enable DST When DST is used in the region where the syslog server is
located, you can enable the DST.
After the setting, when the time zone where the syslog
server is located is in the DST period, the forwarded syslog
packets contain DST identifiers.
Encoding format You can set the encoding format of syslog packets to be
forwarded as required. UTF-8 and GBK are supported. The
default encoding format is UTF-8.
After this parameter is set, the syslog packets are forwarded
in the selected encoding format.
Step 5 Click OK.
On the Forwarding Server page, check the syslog server information, and check
the connection between the system and the syslog server.
● If Connection Status is Normal, the system is successfully interconnected
with the syslog server.
● If Connection Status is Unknown, it is uncertain whether the syslog server
has been successfully interconnected. In this situation, click Refresh later to
check the latest status.
● If Connection Status is Disconnected, the system fails to interconnect with
the syslog server. In this situation, check the parameter settings. If the
parameter settings are correct, rectify the fault by referring to the alarm
"Alarm of the Failure to Connect the Master and Standby Syslog Servers".
Step 6 Optional: Set the number of times and interval for checking the connectivity of
the syslog server as required.
1. In the navigation pane, choose Forwarding Rule.
2. On the Forwarding Rule page, enable Forwarding Server Connectivity
Check
3. Set the number of times and interval for checking the connectivity of the
syslog server as required.

NO TE
● This function takes effect only when both the active and standby syslog servers are
configured. By default, this function is disabled. For details about the check principle,
see 8.2.4.1.7 Principles.
● If this function is enabled and the active and standby syslog servers are configured, the
system periodically checks whether the connectivity of the active syslog server is normal
at the configured interval. If the connectivity of the active syslog server is normal, logs
are forwarded to the active syslog server. If the number of check times reaches the
specified value and the connectivity of the active syslog server is still abnormal, the
system then checks the connectivity of the standby syslog server at the configured
interval. If the connectivity of the standby syslog server is normal, the system forwards
logs to the standby syslog server and reports the alarm "Alarm of the Switchover to the
Standby Syslog Server." If the number of check times reaches the specified value and the
connectivity of the standby syslog server is still abnormal, logs are not forwarded. In this
case, the alarm "Alarm of the Failure to Connect the Master and Standby Syslog
Servers" is reported.
----End
Related Tasks
● Viewing syslog server information
– On the Forwarding Server page, view Active Server IP Address, Active
Server Port, Transfer protocol, Connection Status, Enable Status, and
Operation.
– Click on the left of the desired IP address to view Standby server IP
address, Standby server port, Syslog protocol, Syslog level, Required
string, and Remarks.
● Modifying a syslog server
On the Forwarding Server page, click the IP address of an active server. On
the page that is displayed, modify the syslog server information.
● Deleting syslog servers
– To delete a single syslog server, click Delete in the Operation column.
– To delete multiple syslog servers, select the servers and click Delete
above the list.
● To ensure successful interconnection, the encryption algorithms and protocols
used by the NetEco server and syslog server must be the same. If different
encryption algorithms or protocols are used, you need to add or delete the
encryption algorithms used by the NetEco server.
NO TE
For the communication security of the system, you are advised to interconnect the
system with the syslog server that uses a secure encryption algorithm and protocol.
Change the encryption algorithms or protocols as follows:
a. Choose System > System Management > Application Security from the
main menu.
b. On the Configuration Check page, click the protocol type name of the
log forwarding server in the Service Configuration Item column.
c. On the details panel, modify Configuration Item as required.

After syslog servers are configured and interconnected, logs will be forwarded as
described in 8.2.4.1.7 Principles.
8.2.4.3 Configuring a Log Forwarding Rule
On the Forwarding Rule page, you can set the levels and format for log
forwarding. Logs at levels intersected between Forwarding Level on the
Forwarding Rule page and Syslog level on the Forwarding Server page will be
forwarded.
Prerequisites
You have the Log Transfer Management permission.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Log Forwarding Setting from the
main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Forwarding Rule.
Step 3 On the Forwarding Rule page, click the desired levels for different types of logs,
and set the forwarding format.
Step 4 Optional: Enable Forwarding Server Connectivity Check as required. For details,
see Step 6.
Step 5 Click Apply.
Logs at levels intersected between Forwarding Level on the current page and
Syslog level on the Forwarding Server page will be forwarded. For details, see
8.2.4.4.1 Basic Q&A.
----End

8.2.4.4.2 Why Are Some Logs Lost on the Syslog Server?
Question
I selected a log level when configuring the log forwarding rule or syslog server, but
the syslog server does not receive logs at that level. Why?
Answer
The system can forward logs using UDP, TCP, or TLS. If UDP is used, logs may be
lost. You are advised to use TCP or TLS.

8.2.4.4.3 Why Cannot the Log Forwarding Function Work?
Question
Why cannot I find logs on the configured Syslog server?
Answer
● Contact the system administrator to check the network between the Syslog
server and the log forwarding service.
● If UDP is used to forward logs, check whether the IP address and port number
of the Syslog server are correctly set.
a. Choose System > System Management > Log Forwarding Setting from
the main menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Forwarding Server.
c. In the forwarding server list, click the IP address of the active Syslog
server.
d. Check and modify server information as required.
● If TCP or TLS is used to forward logs, check whether the Syslog server is
properly connected.
NO TE
For security purposes, the system uses TLSv1.3 by default.
a. Choose System > System Management > Log Forwarding Setting from
the main menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Forwarding Server.
c. Check whether Connection Status is Normal.
If Connection Status is Unknown or Disconnected, the Syslog server is

is correct but Connection Status is Unknown or Disconnected, contact
technical support.
● The log forwarding function is disabled.
Contact technical support to check whether the log forwarding function is
enabled. If it is disabled, enable it.

8.2.6 User Policy Management
User policies allow you to set access control rules for users. This function improves
O&M efficiency and prevents unauthorized users from accessing the system to
ensure system security.
8.2.6.1 Setting the Account Policy
The account policy provides a control mechanism used by the system to ensure
account security. If the enabled items in the account policy do not meet security
requirements, security administrators can set login or lockout policy items for user
accounts as required to improve system access security. The account policy takes
effect for all users.

You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Context
To improve account security, you are advised to enable all the items in the account
policy.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Account Policy.
Step 3 On the Account Policy page, set the account policy. Some of the policy
parameters are described in Table 8-36.
Table 8-36 Parameter description
Parameter Description

suspension no login within a period are enabled, the system
automatically disables or deletes the non-default users that
Enable the user
meet the policies.

and does not take effect for third-party users. If this

automatically logged out.

Show warning This parameter specifies the rules that users must comply
upon with. The warning message is displayed after a user logs in to
successful login the system, which serves as a legal statement. Security
administrators can customize this warning message based on
user management regulation.
Step 4 Click Apply.
----End
8.2.6.2 Setting the Password Policy
A password policy provides a control mechanism that ensures password

complexity, change interval, and character restrictions as required to prevent users
from setting weak passwords or using a password for a long period of time,
improving system access security. The password policy takes effect for all users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Context
● Disabling the password complexity policy items and the item of forcible
password change upon expiration will reduce user account security. You are
advised to enable all provided items in the password policy.
● Periodically changing passwords can improve user information security and
prevent accounts from being stolen. Exercise caution when disabling the
function of periodically changing passwords and modifying a password
change interval.
● A new password policy does not affect the existing passwords.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Password Policy.
Step 3 Set the password policy. Some of the policy parameters are described in Table
8-37.
Table 8-37 Parameter description
Parameter Description
Min. password usage After Min. password usage period (days) or Enforce
period (days) password expiration is enabled, if Compulsory
password renewal is also enabled in Advanced
Enforce password
Settings on the Basic Information tab page,
expiration
Compulsory password renewal in Advanced Settings
takes effect preferentially.
To ensure the security of all user accounts in the system,
setting user passwords to never expire is not supported.
If there are related service requirements, you can set a
single user's password to never expire as follows:
1. Choose System > System Management > User
Management from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Users. Click the
username of the desired user.
3. On the Basic Information tab, click Edit. In the
Advanced Settings area, enable Compulsory
password renewal and set Password validity (days)
to 0.

Parameter Description
Force logout upon When a user password is reset, all online sessions
password reset generated by the user will be logged out.
Min. characters This parameter specifies the minimum number of
different between positions with a character different between the old and
new & old passwords new password.
Password cannot be When setting a user password, the password must meet
an increasing, the following requirements:
decreasing, or
● The password cannot be digits or letters in ascending
interval sequence of
or descending sequence.
digits or letters
For example, the password cannot be abcdef, fedcba,
123456, or 654321.
● The password cannot contain an ascending or
descending arithmetic sequence in consecutive odd
positions or even positions.
For example, the password cannot be 1a2a3a,
5a3a1a, a2b2c2, or 2e2c2a.
Password cannot The password dictionary records weak password strings.
contain words in This policy is deselected by default.
password dictionary
● The password dictionary can be customized. For
example, abcd1234 is a weak password and if it is
added to the password dictionary, abcd1234 cannot
be used as a user password.
● In a password dictionary file, passwords are separated
by newline characters. In the password dictionary, a
password that contains more than 128 characters is
invalid.
If this policy item is selected, when you create a user and
set the password, the password cannot contain any
string in the password dictionary. Updating the password
dictionary will replace the existing password dictionary
in the system. The procedure is as follows:
1. Click Download to download the existing password
dictionary and modify it as required.
2. Click the folder icon and select the modified password
dictionary.
3. Click Upload to update the password dictionary.

Parameter Description
Convert strings in The string conversion rules define the rules for
password based on converting a character or string into an uppercase or
conversion rules lowercase letter. The escaped password must meet the
(refer to help requirements of the current password policy. The string
documentation to conversion rules file can contain multiple rules. Each rule
configure the rules) occupies a line and must meet the following
requirements:
NOTE
This parameter is ● In each rule, the strings on the left side of the last
displayed only when
equal sign (=) are converted to the first letter on the
Password cannot
right side. For example, |-|1234=a = x indicates that
contain words in
|-|1234=a is converted into x.
password dictionary
is selected. ● The spaces, newline characters, carriage returns, and
tab characters at the beginning and end of the strings
on the left and right sides of the last equal sign are
filtered out. For example, x=x = y indicates that x=x is
converted to y.
● The first character (after spaces, newline characters,
carriage returns, and tab characters are filtered out)
on the right side of the last equal sign must be a
letter. For example, x=x = 2ya is an invalid rule.
● If there are multiple rules with the same string on the
left side of the equal sign, they are executed in
sequence, so only the last rule takes effect.
If this policy item is selected, when a password contains
a string defined in the string conversion rules, the string
is converted into another string based on defined rules.
Updating the string conversion rules overwrites existing
rules, and invalid rules are filtered out. You can update
the rules as follows:
1. Click Download to download the existing string
conversion rules and modify them as required.
2. Click the folder icon and select the modified file.
3. Click Upload to update the string conversion rules.
Example: If a rule for converting the string |-|1234 a into
x exists in the string conversion rules, and xbcd!123 is
contained in the password dictionary, and when the
policy item is selected, |-|1234 abcd!123 cannot be used
as a user password.
Step 4 Click Apply for the password policy to take effect.
----End
8.2.6.3 Setting a Client IP Address Policy
A client IP address policy provides a control mechanism for checking the

specified users. The policies take effect only for the bound users. In this way, users
can log in to the management system only from specified IP address segments,
which improves system security.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > User Policies.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Client IP Address Policies.
Step 3 On the Client IP Address Policies page, click Create.
Step 4 Set a client IP address policy and click OK.
NO TICE
Exercise caution when you set the client IP address policies for the system
administrator user. If the client IP address of the system administrator user is not
within the bound IP address range, the user cannot log in again after logout.
----End
8.2.6.4 Setting a Login Time Policy
A login time policy provides a control mechanism for checking the validity time of
an external access request during system operation. Security administrators can
set login time policies as required and bind the policies to specified users. The
policies take effect only for the bound users. In this way, users can log in to the
management system only within the specified time segments, which improves
system security.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > User Policies.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Login Time Policies.
Step 3 On the Login Time Policies page, click Create.
Step 4 Set the parameters of the login time policy and click OK.

NO TE
● The system administrator user is not restricted by the login time policy.
● If the start time is later than the end time for Effective hours, the system will
automatically set the end time to the next day. For example, if Effective hours is set to
16:00–15:00, login is allowed within 16:00–24:00 today and 0:00–15:00 the next day.
----End
8.2.6.5 Setting Verification Code Policies
Security administrators can set verification code policies as required to improve
system access security. The verification policies apply to all users.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > User Policies.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Verification Code Policy.
Step 3 On the Verification Code Policy page, configure the policies.
Step 4 Click Apply.
----End
8.2.7 Configuring Secondary Authorization Operations
To restrict user operations that are dangerous or have great impact, security
administrators can configure secondary authorization policies for these operations.
This improves system security.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Secondary Authorization from the
main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose an operation type. Select the name of the
operation for which the policy needs to be configured.
Step 3 Click Enable in the Operation column of the row that contains the operation you
want to enable.

NO TE
● Users with secondary authorization rights cannot perform secondary authorization on
themselves.
● The system administrator user is not restricted by secondary authorization policies.
● When secondary authorization is enabled, secondary authorization is required when
operations are performed on pages that support this function.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
After configuring secondary authorization operations, assign the secondary
authorization personnel with the operation rights for Secondary Authorization
Authentication so that they can authenticate operations that require secondary
authorization.
NO TE
A global user with the user management permission can modify the configuration
parameters of global secondary authorization.
8.2.8 Authentication
Authentication provides the functions of managing user rights and NetEco security
policies. These functions can prevent unauthorized users from performing
malicious operations on the NetEco, ensuring data security of the NetEco.
8.2.8.1 Setting the System Login Mode
Multi-user mode and single-user mode are available for system login. In most
cases, the system runs in multi-user mode. If you need to perform maintenance
operations on the NetEco (for example, adjusting the role to which a user is
attached, managed objects, or permissions), switch to the single-user mode to
prevent other users' operations from affecting the system maintenance operations.
Prerequisites
You have logged in to the NetEco as the system administrator user.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main menu.
Step 2 On the System Login Mode page, select a login mode.

NO TE
● Switching to Single-user mode will log out all the sessions except your session.
Therefore, exercise caution when performing this operation. However, third-party users
can log in.
● If Single-user mode is selected, you can set Switching duration. The value 0 indicates
that the system immediately switches to the single-user mode. Other values indicate
that the system switches to the single-user mode after the specified period of time.
● You are advised to switch the system to the multi-user mode immediately after finishing
maintenance operations so that other users can use the system.
Step 3 Click Apply.
----End
8.2.8.2 Remote Authentication Configuration
If remote authentication is enabled, you can interconnect the system with a third-
party system by configuring an AAA protocol. After the interconnection, users are
remotely authenticated by the third-party system instead of the local system. This
section describes how to configure and enable remote authentication.
8.2.8.2.1 About Remote Authentication
This section describes the definition, principles, and protocol types of remote
authentication.
User Authentication Mode
The user authentication modes include local authentication and remote
authentication. In remote authentication mode, users are authenticated by an AAA
server through AAA protocols. The NetEco supports Lightweight Directory Access
Protocol (LDAP) and Remote Authentication Dial In User Service (RADIUS) for
AAA authentication. For details, see Table 8-38. The LDAP authentication mode is
similar to the RADIUS authentication mode.
Table 8-38 User authentication modes
Authentication Authentication Protocol Description
Mode
Local N/A During the NetEco login,
authentication user authentication is
implemented by a local
server through user
management. After local
authentication is enabled,
remote authentication is
automatically disabled.

Authentication Authentication Protocol Description
Mode
Rem LDAP LDAP stands for Lightweight During the NetEco login,
ote authentic Directory Access Protocol. The user authentication is
aut ation system communicates with the implemented by the LDAP
hent LDAP server in common mode server.
icati (without encryption), or
on. Transport Layer Security (TLS)
mode.
RADIUS RADIUS is defined in the RFC During the NetEco login,
authentic 2865 and RFC 2866 specifications user authentication is
ation and is the most widely used AAA implemented by the
protocol. RADIUS supports the RADIUS server.
following authentication modes:

(PAP), Challenge Handshake

(MS-CHAPv1), Microsoft

(MS-CHAPv2), and two-factor
authentication. The system
communicates with the RADIUS
server using User Datagram
Protocol (UDP), Transmission
Control Protocol (TCP), or TLS.
Principles of LDAP and RADIUS Authentication
Figure 8-17 shows the interconnection between the NetEco and an AAA server.

1. A user enters their username and password on the local PC to log in to the
NetEco.
2. The NetEco sends the username and password to the LDAP or RADIUS server
for authentication.
3. After the authentication is successful, the LDAP or RADIUS server returns the
user information to the NetEco. Then, the user successfully logs in to the
NetEco.
8.2.8.2.2 Configuring LDAP Authentication
LDAP stands for Lightweight Directory Access Protocol. It supports multiple
authentication modes. Currently, the system supports common communication
(without encryption) and communication protected by TLS certificate
authentication. Users can set login information on the LDAP server, and user
authentication during the login is performed by the LDAP server.

NO TICE
The security mechanisms for remote authentication depend on the third-party
server. Security mechanisms, such as anti-brute force cracking, password
complexity check, and anti-DoS attack, must be enabled on the third-party server.
● If the anti-brute force cracking mechanism is not enabled, the passwords may
be cracked by brute force through a large number of guesses, leading to
information leakage.
● If the password complexity check mechanism is not enabled, no restriction is
posed on account passwords. Weak passwords may result in breached accounts,
leading to account information leakage.
● If the anti-DoS attack mechanism is not enabled, the system is vulnerable to
attacks of highly frequent requests initiated by abnormal means. In such case,
the system resources will be exhausted, and the system cannot properly provide
services.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
● The LDAP server has been installed and correctly configured and can
communicate with the nodes where the security service resides.
● You have obtained the information about the LDAP server from the LDAP
server provider.
● The parameters required for LDAP authentication have been configured. For
details, see 8.2.8.2.4 LDAP Authentication Parameter Description.
● The username case sensitivity on the NetEco has been configured to be the
same as that on the LDAP server, if the username on the LDAP server is case-
sensitive. For details, see "Setting Service Parameters for User Management"
in Administrator Guide.
● The LDAP certificate has been uploaded in certificate management, if Enable
TLS need to be selected.
Context
After remote authentication is enabled, a remote user cannot log in to the NetEco
in the following situations:
● The remote user and the default user in the system share the same username.
● The username of the remote user does not meet the setting rule of
Username.
After remote authentication is enabled, remote user groups fail to be synchronized
to the local system in the following situations:
● The remote user group name does not meet the naming rule in Role. For
example, the name length exceeds 64 characters, or the name contains
escape characters, invisible characters, or special characters `~*()|[]
{}:,+;="'<>/?\
● The remote user group exists in the mapping table of Use remotely stored

system.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose Remote Authentication.
Step 3 On the Remote Authentication page, click LDAP Authentication.
Step 4 Enter basic information.
1. Set the address type, address, and port number of the LDAP server. If the
LDAP servers are deployed in active/standby mode, set the address types,
addresses, and port numbers of the active and standby servers. A maximum
of four standby servers can be configured.
2. Set the communication mode between the LDAP server and the system server.
If Enable TLS is selected, select the corresponding protocol version and
upload the LDAP certificate in certificate management. For data security
during communication, you are advised to enable TLS.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Management from the main
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Service Certificate Management.
c. On the Services page, click LDAP.
d. Import certificate files on the corresponding tab pages.
Step 5 Set server information.
1. Set User authentication mode and enter related information.
2. Set parameters in User Attributes and User Group Attributes based on the
server information.
3. Set parameters in User-to-User Group Bindings and other parameters based
on service requirements.
Step 6 Click Test. In the Test Connection dialog box, enter the username and password
of the remote user on the LDAP server.
Perform operations according to the test result. For details, see Table 8-39.
Table 8-39 Test results and operations
Test Operation
Result
Connectivi If Use remotely stored bindings is used, check whether the value

● If not, rectify the fault based on the possible causes and then

Step 7 Click Apply for the LDAP authentication settings to take effect.
Step 8 Optional: If User authentication mode is set to Fixed user, click Synchronize
User Group to synchronize user groups from the remote server to the local server.
----End
Related Tasks
If the encryption algorithm configured on the local system is not supported by the
LDAP server, you need to add or delete algorithms on the local system.
NO TE
For security purposes, you are advised to use the LDAP server with a secure encryption
algorithm and use secure protocols for data transfer.
The procedure is as follows:
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Application Security from the main
menu.
Step 2 On the Configuration Check page, click the Remote Authentication-LDAP
Authentication check item.
Step 3 On the details panel, modify Configuration Item as required.
Step 4 Click Save.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
After setting the remote authentication, log out of the NetEco and then log in as a
remote user to verify the remote authentication interconnection.
8.2.8.2.3 Configuring RADIUS Authentication
RADIUS is the most widely used AAA protocol and is defined in the RFC2865 and

underlying communication modes supported by the system are UDP, TCP, and TLS.
NO TICE
The security mechanisms for remote authentication depend on the third-party
server. Security mechanisms, such as anti-brute force cracking, password
complexity check, and anti-DoS attack, must be enabled on the third-party server.
● If the anti-brute force cracking mechanism is not enabled, the passwords may
be cracked by brute force through a large number of guesses, leading to
information leakage.
● If the password complexity check mechanism is not enabled, no restriction is
posed on account passwords. Weak passwords may result in breached accounts,
leading to account information leakage.
● If the anti-DoS attack mechanism is not enabled, the system is vulnerable to
attacks of highly frequent requests initiated by abnormal means. In such case,
the system resources will be exhausted, and the system cannot properly provide
services.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
● The RADIUS server has been installed and correctly configured and can
communicate with the nodes where the security service resides.
● You have obtained the RADIUS server information from the RADIUS server
provider to configure RADIUS authentication for the system. For details about
the parameters, see 8.2.8.2.5 RADIUS Authentication Parameter
Description.
● In Two-factor authentication mode, SecurID token has been set on the
RADIUS server.
● The username case sensitivity on the NetEco has been configured to be the
same as that on the RADIUS server, if the username on the RADIUS server is
case-sensitive. For details, see "Setting Service Parameters for User
Management" in Administrator Guide.
● The RADIUS certificate has been uploaded in certificate management, if
Enable TLS need to be selected.
Context
After remote authentication is enabled, a remote user cannot log in to the NetEco
in the following situations:
● The remote user and the default user in the system share the same username.
● The username of the remote user does not meet the setting rule of
Username.
Procedure

Step 3 On the Remote Authentication page, click RADIUS Authentication.
Step 4 Enter basic information.
1. Set the address type, address, and port number of the RADIUS server.
NO TE
– If the RADIUS server is deployed in active/standby mode, enter the address types,
addresses, and port numbers of both the active and standby servers.
– The NetEco uses the IP address of the node where the user process of SM resides
or where SMLogLic resides to communicate with the RADIUS server. For details
about how to obtain the IP address of the node where a service resides, see
"Querying the Management IP Address of the Node Where a Process or Service
Resides" in Administrator Guide.
2. Set the communication mode between the RADIUS server and the system
server.
If Enable TLS is selected, select the corresponding protocol version and
upload the RADIUS certificate in certificate management. For data security
during communication, you are advised to enable TLS.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Management from the main
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Service Certificate Management.
c. On the Services page, click RADIUS.
d. Import certificate files on the corresponding tab pages.
3. Set other information such as the authentication mode.
– If Authentication mode is set to CHAP, PAP, MS-CHAPv1, or MS-
CHAPv2, enter the username and password of the remote user for
testing. The password is the account password.
– If Authentication mode is set to Two-factor, enter the username and
password of the remote user for testing. The password consists of the PIN
and token code.
▪
The PIN is a string of 4 to 8 digits or letters. The initial value must be
obtained from the RADIUS server provider. Reset the PIN when
performing the first test as the user.
▪
The token code is a 6-digit number generated by the RSA SecurID
hardware device. The RSA SecurID hardware device needs to be
obtained from the RADIUS server provider.
Step 5 Enter other RADIUS authentication information.
Step 6 Click Test. In the Test Connection dialog box, enter information about the remote
user on the RADIUS server.
Perform operations according to the test result. For details, see Table 8-40.

Test Result Operation
Connectivit If Obtain the user group binding relationship from default
y test attributes or Obtain the user group binding relationship from
successful. user-defined attributes is used, check whether the value of User
group or Default operation set in the displayed information is

● If not, rectify the fault based on the possible causes, and then

Step 7 Click Apply for the RADIUS authentication settings to take effect.
----End
Related Tasks
If the encryption algorithm configured on the local system is not supported by the
RADIUS server, you need to add or delete algorithms on the local system.
NO TE
For security purposes, you are advised to use the RADIUS server with secure encryption
algorithms and to use secure protocols for data transfer.
The procedure is as follows:
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Application Security from the main
menu.
Step 2 On the Configuration Check page, click the Remote Authentication-RADIUS
Authentication check item.
Step 3 On the details panel, modify Configuration Item as required.
Step 4 Click Save.

After setting the remote authentication, log out of the NetEco and then log in as a
remote user to verify the remote authentication interconnection.
8.2.8.2.4 LDAP Authentication Parameter Description
This section describes the parameters required for configuring remote LDAP
authentication. For actual parameter configurations, contact LDAP server
maintenance personnel.
● In Basic Information, you can configure the LDAP server address type, server
address, and port number. For details about the parameters, see Table 8-41.
● In User authentication mode, you can set the LDAP authentication mode.
For details, see Table 8-42.
● In User Attributes, you can configure the user root DN, user object class
name, and user unique ID in the LDAP server schema. For details about the
parameters, see Table 8-43.
● In User Group Attributes, you can configure the root DN of user groups,
object class name of user groups, and unique user group ID in the LDAP
server schema. For details about the parameters, see Table 8-44.
● In User-to-User Group Bindings, you can configure the binding relationship
between users and local or remote user groups. For details about the
parameters, see Table 8-45.
● In Advanced Settings, you can configure whether local user authentication is
supported, whether local user management is supported, and whether users
who are not bound to any user group are allowed to log in after LDAP
authentication is enabled. For details about the parameters, see Table 8-46.

the same name, you need to configure the mapping
between the remote user group and the local role. After
the mapping is configured, the remote user is bound to
the local role mapping to the remote user group. If this
parameter is not set, the remote user will lose the
authorization of the user group.

authenticated on the LDAP server.
NOTE
● If the username of a user on the LDAP server is the
same as a user on the local system, the user with the
same username is authenticated locally. You are advised
not to create remote users with the same username as
local users.
● If a third-party user is automatically created when a
remote server user logs in to the system by calling the
interface, the user is always authenticated on the
remote server. If the password for the user is changed
locally, the password on the remote server becomes
invalid, and the user is authenticated locally.
● If Local user authentication is not selected for LDAP
authentication, the system administrator user is
authenticated locally, and the third-party users and
remote users are authenticated on the LDAP server.
– Users on the LDAP server can log in to the web
client only in one mode. If an LDAP user logs in
successfully by calling an interface, the user
cannot log in on the login page, and vice versa.
– If the username of a user on the LDAP server is
the same as a user on the local system, after the
remote user logs in, the attribute of the local user
with the same username is changed, and the local
user may fail to log in or the user permission may
change. Therefore, you are advised not to create
remote users with the same username as local
users.
– When the user on the LDAP server logs in
through the login page, the type of the local
user with the same username is changed to
remote user.
– When the user on the LDAP server logs in by
calling an interface, the type of the local user
with the same username is changed to third-
party user.

8.2.8.2.5 RADIUS Authentication Parameter Description
This section describes the parameters required for configuring remote RADIUS
authentication. For actual parameter configurations, contact RADIUS server
maintenance personnel.
● In Basic Information, you can configure the RADIUS server address type,
server address, and port number. For details, see Table 8-47.
● In Accounting, you can set the accounting ports of the active and standby
servers. For details, see Table 8-48.
● In Request Message, you can set whether the request sent from the client to
the RADIUS server contains the client IP address, local IP address, and local
identifier. For details, see Table 8-49.
● In Response Message, you can set whether to synchronize the mobile
number format and user group binding relationship from the RADIUS server
after a user logs in. For details, see Table 8-50.
NO TE
For the definitions of the attributes in the request and response messages, see the RFC
2865 and RFC 2866 specifications of RADIUS.

8.2.8.3 CAS SSO Configuration
System administrators can configure the CAS SSO server and clients on the GUI to
implement the CAS SSO function.
8.2.8.3.1 About CAS SSO
This section describes the definition and principles of CAS SSO.
CAS SSO allows SSO login complying with the CAS protocol. In this way, an SSO
server is connected to multiple SSO clients to perform unified authentication. After
CAS SSO is configured, users can log in to all the clients without entering the
username and password after logging in to the server.
After CAS SSO is configured, users can log in to other clients without entering the
username and password after logging in to one of the clients. When a user logs in
to the client, the user is automatically mapped to and attached to the roles that
have the same names with the roles to which the user is attached on the server.
To ensure that the user has the same permissions on the client, you are advised to
create the same roles and permissions on the client and the server. If a role that
the user is attached to does not exist on the client, the user will lose the
permissions of the role on the client. Assume that system 1 is the SSO server, and
system 2, system 3, ..., and system N are SSO clients. Figure 8-18 shows the SSO
configuration.

System configurations:
1. Configure system 1 as the SSO server and configure the trust addresses of the
SSO clients on the SSO Servers tab page of system 1. Specifically, set the IP
addresses of system 2, system 3, ..., and system N as trusted addresses.
2. Configure system 2, system 3, ..., and system N as SSO clients. Set
Authentication URL and Validation URL of the SSO server (the IP address of
system 1) on the SSO Clients tab page of system 2, system 3, ..., and system
N.
User access:
1. To access client 1 for the first time, a user enters the IP address or domain
name of client 1 in the address box of a browser, presses Enter, and enters
the username and password.
2. The user information is sent to the SSO server for login authentication.
3. The SSO server authenticates the username and password and sends an
authentication success message to client 1.
4. The user logs in to client 1 successfully and is attached to the roles with same
names on client 1.
5. To access client 2 for the first time, a user enters the IP address or domain

7. The user logs in to client 2 successfully and is attached to the roles with same
names on client 2.
8.2.8.3.2 Configuring CAS SSO
CAS SSO configuration consists of SSO Servers and SSO Clients. In SSO mode,
you can log in to all other mutually trusted clients without entering the username
and password again after logging in to a client.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the NetEco as a security administrator.
● This section uses the configuration of the current system as an example. The
configurations of other systems are also required. The CAS SSO is successfully
configured only after all the systems are configured with CAS SSO.
For example, if the system is deployed in active/standby mode, you need to
perform the configuration on both the active and standby systems.
● The nodes where the security service resides can communicate with the peer
system.
Context
● Before you configure server parameters on CAS SSO clients, ensure that the
required trust certificate has been imported to the CAS SSO clients. Otherwise,
you cannot log in to the NetEco.
● If remote authentication has been enabled for a system, you are advised not
to use this system as a CAS SSO client.
● After SSO is configured, no local users on the client can log in to the NetEco,
and a server user cannot log in to the NetEco in the following situations:
– The username of the server user is the same as the username of a third-
party user or a default user other than the system administrator user.
– The server username does not meet the setting rule of Username.
Procedure
● If the current system functions as the CAS SSO server, you need to configure
SSO client addresses on the SSO Servers tab page.
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose SSO Configuration > CAS SSO Configuration.
Step 3 On the SSO Servers tab page, click Create.
Step 4 Set the trusted address, that is, the IP address or domain name, of the CAS SSO
client interconnected with the system
If the CAS SSO client is deployed in active/standby mode, configure the trusted
address of the standby system to improve interconnection stability.
Step 5 Click OK.

SSO server addresses on the SSO Clients tab page.
Step 1 Before configuring the CAS SSO client, upload the trust certificate.
1. Contact the administrator to obtain the ER certificate trust.cer of the CAS
SSO server, which is the trust certificate of the CAS SSO server.
NO TE
If the CAS SSO server is deployed in active/standby mode and the trust certificates of
the active and standby systems are different, you need to obtain the trust certificates
of both the active and standby systems of the server.
2. Upload the trust certificate on the CAS SSO client.
a. Choose System > About > Certificate Management from the main
menu.
b. In the navigation pane, choose Service Certificate Management.
c. On the Services page, click UnisessionService.
d. On the Trust Certificates tab page, import the trust certificate.
Step 2 Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main menu.
Step 3 In the navigation pane, choose SSO Configuration > CAS SSO Configuration.
Step 4 On the SSO Clients tab page, click Edit and toggle on Status.
Step 5 Set Authentication URL and Validation URL. The addresses are the IP addresses
or domain names of the CAS SSO server.
● If the CAS SSO server does not distinguish the authentication server from the
verification server, the values of Authentication URL and Validation URL are
the same.
● If the CAS SSO server distinguishes the authentication server from the
verification server, the values of Authentication URL and Validation URL are
different.
● If the CAS SSO server is deployed in active/standby mode, configure Backup
authentication URL and Backup validation URL for the standby system to
improve system stability.
Step 6 Set Allowed regions. This parameter specifies the regions to which the server
users who are allowed to access the current client belong. Users of the default
region are not restricted by the parameter.
If values are specified for this parameter, only the users of the specified regions
can log in to the client. If this parameter is left blank, all users are allowed to
access the client.
NO TE
This function can be used only when both the CAS SSO server and client support it.
Otherwise, the function may be abnormal.
Step 7 Click OK.
----End
● After configuring the CAS SSO on all the systems, check whether the CAS SSO

and press Enter.
The IP address in the address box of the browser is automatically converted to the
IP address of the CAS SSO server.
Step 2 Enter the username and password. After the authentication is successful, you will
successfully log in to client 1.
NO TE
When a server user logs in to the client, if the username of the server user is the same as
that of a local user on the client, the user type of the local user is changed to remote user.
As a result, the user with the same username on the client cannot log in to the system.
Therefore, it is recommended that the username of the server user be different from that of
any local user on the client.
Step 3 When the session of client 1 is valid, enter the IP address or domain name of
client 2 in the address box on another tab page of the same browser and press
Enter. Automatic login to client 2 is successful.
----End
Related Tasks
● Viewing CAS SSO client configurations: When Status is Enabled, the client
configurations are effective. When Status is Disabled, the client
configurations do not take effect.
● Deleting CAS SSO configurations:
Step 1 On the CAS SSO client, disable the client configuration.
1. Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main
menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose CAS SSO Configuration.
3. On the SSO Clients tab page, click Edit and toggle off Status.
4. Click OK.
NO TE
If the user type of a local user on the client is changed because the username is the
same as that of a server user, the user type of the local user cannot be restored after
the SSO configuration is disabled. As a result, such users cannot log in to the client.
You are advised to create new accounts for these users.
Step 2 On the CAS SSO server, disable the server configuration.
1. Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main
menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose SSO Configuration > CAS SSO Configuration.
3. On the SSO Servers tab page, click Delete in the Operation column of the
row that contains the trusted IP address to be deleted; alternatively, select
multiple trusted IP addresses and click Delete to the upper right corner of the
list to delete them.

not supported by the CAS SSO server, you need to add or delete TLS protocol
versions or encryption algorithms of the CAS SSO client to ensure that the
CAS SSO interconnection is successful.
NO TE
For security purposes, you are advised to use the CAS SSO server with secure
encryption algorithms and to use secure protocols for data transfer.
The operations are as follows:
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Application Security from the main
menu.
Step 2 On the Configuration Check page, click the SSO Configuration-CAS check item.
Step 3 On the details panel, modify Configuration Item as required.
Step 4 Click Save.
----End
8.2.8.4 SAML SSO Configuration
Security administrators can configure the SAML SSO server and clients on the web
client to implement the SAML SSO function.
8.2.8.4.1 About SAML SSO
This section describes the definition and principles of SAML SSO.
SAML SSO consists of IdPs and SPs.
● IdP: A system that collects and stores user identity information, such as
usernames and passwords, and authenticates users when they log in to the
system.
● SP: A system that establishes a trust relationship with the IdP and uses the
user information provided by the IdP to provide services for users.
When users want to use their IdPs and the functions provided by the NetEco, they
can configure the NetEco as the SP on the IdP Configuration tab page. In this
way, they can use the IdP accounts to log in to the NetEco in SSO mode.
When users want to use the NetEco as an IdP to interconnect the NetEco with
their SPs, they can configure the NetEco as the IdP on the SP Configuration tab
page. In this way, they can use the NetEco accounts to log in to the SPs in SSO
mode.
After the SAML SSO system is configured, users can log in to the IdP from Login
URL of the SP without entering the username and password repeatedly. Figure
8-19 shows the SAML SSO interconnection principle between the IdP and SP.

As shown in the preceding figure, the SAML SSO interconnection procedure is as
follows:
1. A user opens the login URL obtained from the SP in the browser. The browser
sends an SSO request to the SP.
2. The browser sends a request containing the IdP identifier to the SP.
3. The SP queries the IdP information based on the IdP identifier and redirects
the request to the IdP server through the browser.
4. The IdP returns the login page to the browser.
5. The user enters the username and password for authentication.
6. The IdP authenticates the user information and sends an authentication
success message to the browser.
7. The user logs in to the SP in SSO mode and accesses the SP based on the
permissions.
If the SP also functions as an IdP or server to interconnect with another level-3
system, or the IdP (or the CAS SSO server) also functions as an SP to interconnect
with another IdP, that is, the current system functions as the intermediate system
to interconnect with an upper- or lower-level system in SSO mode, the cascading
SSO mode is formed. Figure 8-20 shows the principles of the cascading SSO
interconnection, and the user login methods are described in Table 8-51.
NO TE
In the SSO cascading scenario, the NetEco can be configured as a system of any level.

In detail:
(1) Configure the SSO interconnection between the level-1 and level-2 systems. In
SAML SSO mode, the level-1 system can be configured as the IdP, and the level-2
system can be configured as the SP. For details, see 8.2.8.4.2 Configuring SAML
SSO.
(2) Configure the SSO interconnection between the level-2 and level-3 systems. In
SAML SSO mode, configure the level-2 system as the IdP, and the level-3 system
as the SP. For details, see 8.2.8.4.2 Configuring SAML SSO.
(3) Configure the SSO interconnection between the level-2 and level-3 systems. In
CAS SSO mode, configure the level-2 system as the server, and the level-3 system
as the client. For details, see 8.2.8.3.2 Configuring CAS SSO.

Login Login Process
Method
Login Login method for the level-1 system, which is applicable only to
method level-1 system users.
1
Login Login method for the level-2 system, which is applicable in the
method following cases:
2
● A level-1 system user uses one of the methods in Other Login
Methods to be authenticated on the level-1 system, and then the
user can log in the level-2 system.
● Level-2 system users can directly log in to the level-2 system.
Login Login method for the level-3 system, which is applicable in the
method following cases:
3
● A level-1 user uses one of the methods in Other Login Methods
to be redirected twice to the level-1 system for authentication,
and then the user can log in to the level-3 system.
● A level-2 system user uses one of the methods in Other Login
Methods to be authenticated on a level-2 system, and then the
user can log in the level-3 system.
● Level-3 system users can directly log in to the level-3 system.
8.2.8.4.2 Configuring SAML SSO
This section describes how to configure the SAML SSO. If the current system
functions as an SP, you need to configure the IdP information. Alternatively, if the
current system functions as an IdP, you need to configure the SP information.
Prerequisites
● You have logged in to the NetEco as the system administrator.
● This section uses the configuration of the current system as an example. The
configurations of other systems are also required. The SAML SSO is
successfully configured only after all the systems are configured with SAML
SSO.
For example, if the system is deployed in active/standby mode, you need to
perform the configuration on both the active and standby systems.
● To interconnect with an IdP, you have obtained the IdP metadata file before
configuring the SP. To interconnect with an SP, you have obtained the SP
metadata file before configuring the IdP.
● To interconnect with a third-party IdP, configure the third-party IdP first and
obtain the metadata file of the IdP. For details, see 8.2.8.4.3 Configuring SSO
Interconnection on the IdP System.

After SSO is configured, an IdP user cannot log in to the NetEco in the following
situations:
● The IdP user shares the same username as the SP system administrator.
Therefore, the IdP user becomes the SP system administrator after login.
● Username(SAML) of the IdP user in the SP system does not meet the
username requirements of the SP system.
Processes
Figure 8-21 shows the process of configuring SAML SSO. In the figure, process 1
shows the process of configuring the SP system, and process 2 shows the process
of configuring the IdP system.
Figure 8-21 SAML SSO configuration process
Procedure
● If the current system functions as the IdP, perform the following steps to
configure the SP to be interconnected:

Step 3 On the SP Configuration tab page, click Create and configure the SP information.
Step 4 Configure basic information about the SP.
Step 5 Configure metadata. Click the .
Step 6 Select the SP metadata file and click Upload.
Step 7 Configure the attribute mapping rule based on the requirements of the SP to be
interconnected.

Management from the main menu.
2. In the navigation pane, choose Service Certificate Management.
3. On the Services page, click UniSSOWebsite_SAML_IdP.
4. Import certificate files on the corresponding tab pages.
NO TE
If the system is deployed in active/standby mode and it is inconvenient to log in to the
standby system, you can manually change the IP address in the metadata file exported in
Step 9 to that of the standby system and use the modified file as the IdP metadata file for
the standby system to interconnect with the SP system. For details about how to change
the IP address in the metadata file, see 8.2.8.4.4 Obtaining the Metadata File of the
Standby System.
Step 10 On the SP Configuration tab page, click Export Metadata. The exported IdP
metadata file can be used for configuring the SP.
----End
● If the current system functions as the SP, perform the following steps to
configure the IdP to be interconnected:
Step 1 Choose System > System Management > Authentication from the main menu.
Step 2 In the navigation pane, choose SSO Configuration > SAML SSO Configuration.
Step 3 On the IdP Configuration tab page, click Create and configure the IdP

Step 5 Configure parameters in Advanced Settings. For details about the parameters, see
Table 8-53.
Table 8-53 Parameters in Advanced Settings

Redirect to Whether to redirect a user to the configured IdP when the user

users to log bound to any user group cannot log in. By default, Allow

not bound to any user group can log in.

user policy in the format of username(SAML). If a user of the IdP shares the
same username with a third-party user or local user of the SP, the

login page.

management.

Management from the main menu.

4. Import certificate files on the corresponding tab pages.
