8.5.3 Certificate Authority Service
The CA service offers basic CA capabilities and supports both CMPv2 and privacy
CA protocols. It can replace a third-party CA system for certificate application and
management.
8.5.3.1 Certificate Authority Service Overview
This section provides an overview of the Certificate Authority Service, including its
definition, functions, and application scenarios.

The Certificate Authority Service is used to issue and manage certificates, helping
users quickly obtain and use certificates.
Concepts
● Certificate Authority (CA): An authoritative and impartial third-party
organization responsible for issuing, authenticating, and managing
certificates. A CA is a tree structure consisting of a root CA and multiple
subordinate CAs.
● Certificate Revocation List (CRL): A list of certificates that have been revoked
by the issuing CA before their scheduled expiration date. It is a kind of
certificate blacklist.
● Root CA: The top-level CA in the CA hierarchy. It is the start point of the entire
CA chain of trust. The corresponding CA certificate is self-signed and does not
need to be verified by other CAs.
● Subordinate CA: A certificate authority signed by the root CA or other
subordinate CAs.
NO TE
The root CA issues root certificates using the same 'subject' and 'issuer' names,
whereas the subordinate CA issues subordinate certificates using different 'subject' and
'issuer' names.
● Certificate chain: An ordered list of certificates from multiple levels of CAs. A
certificate chain verifies the certificates issued by the lowest-level CA in the
certificate chain.
NO TE
The issuer of the current CA certificate is the subject of the upper-level CA certificate.
● PKI: public key infrastructure, which is a standards-compliant infrastructure
that adopts public key theory and technology to provide security services.
● End entity: end user of a PKI product or service. It can be an individual, an
organization, a device (such as a router or firewall), or a process running on a
computer.
● End-entity certificate: A certificate that does not use its key to issue other
certificates.
● Initial end entity certificate: A certificate provided by the end user of PKI
products or services. You can apply for a initial end entity certificate from the
Certificate Authority Service through Certificate Management Protocol (CMP).
● Cross-certificate: a certificate used for cross-certification between different
CAs. For example, if there are two CAs: CA1 and CA2, CA1 has issued the
device certificate cacert1, and CA2 has issued the device certificate cacert2,
cacert1 can only be authenticated by CA1, whereas cacert2 can only be
authenticated by CA2. To enable CA1 to authenticate cacert2, export the
public key of CA2 to apply for a CA certificate crosscert2 from CA1, and
crosscert2 is a cross-certificate. In this way, CA1 can authenticate cacert2
through the path CA1 > crosscert2 > cacert2. The cross-certificate is an
intermediate certificate (bridge) that connects CA1 and CA2.
● One-way TLS: A secure communication protocol that uses digital certificates

the server does not verify the client identity certificate.
● Two-way TLS: The server and the client use a trusted certificate to
authenticate each other.
● Online certificate status protocol (OCSP): It is a protocol to query the online
certificate status.
● Endorsement Key (EK): A key generated by the vendor of a Trusted Platform
Module (TPM) chip to uniquely identify the TPM chip. According to the
Trusted Computing Group (TCG) specifications, the TPM stores the EK
certificate issued by a trusted third party to verify the validity of the EK. The
EK is important private information and cannot be used for signature.
● Attestation Key (AK): A key used in remote attestation to prevent EKs from
being disclosed. It is used to sign measurement data (such as the PCR value)
stored in the TPM. An AK certificate is a certificate obtained using the privacy
CA protocol, containing information such as the public AK and CA issuer.
● Certificate Signing Request (CSR): Certificate signing request. In Public Key
Infrastructure (PKI) systems, a certificate signing request is a message sent
from an applicant to a CA in order to apply for a digital certificate. PKCS#10
defines the syntax of a certificate request, which usually contains the public
key for which the certificate should be issued, identifying information (such as
a domain name) and integrity protection (such as a digital signature).
● You can apply for certificates online or offline. The CMP protocol is used to
interact with the CA service in online scenarios, and all scenarios that require
human-machine interaction are offline scenarios. For example, when applying
for a certificate using basic information or through a CSR file on the web UI,
you need to perform related configurations on the web UI.
● The process of digital signing consists of hash and asymmetric encryption.
First, the data to be signed is hashed and the hash value is obtained. Then,
the hash value is encrypted asymmetrically by using the private key of the
signer to obtain the signing result. For details about the signature algorithms,
see Table 8-82.
Table 8-82 Signature algorithms
Signature Description
Algorithm
SHA256withRSA SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
value (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is
an asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric
encryption on the hash value to obtain the final digital
signature value.
SHA384withRSA SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
value (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is
an asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric
encryption on the hash value to obtain the final digital
signature value.

Signature Description
Algorithm
SHA512withRSA SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
value (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. RSA is
an asymmetric algorithm used to perform asymmetric
encryption on the hash value to obtain the final digital
signature value.
SHA256withRSAS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is
SA-PSS more secure than SHA256withRSA.
SHA384withRSAS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is
SA-PSS more secure than SHA384withRSA.
SHA512withRSAS PKCS#1 v2.0 is used for signature padding, which is
SA-PSS more secure than SHA512withRSA.
SHA256withECDS SHA256 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
A value (length: 256 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA
is an asymmetric algorithm used to perform
asymmetric encryption on the hash value to obtain the
final digital signature value.
SHA384withECDS SHA384 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
A value (length: 384 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA
is an asymmetric algorithm used to perform
asymmetric encryption on the hash value to obtain the
final digital signature value.
SHA512withECDS SHA512 is a hash algorithm used to calculate the hash
A value (length: 512 bits) of the data to be signed. ECDSA
is an asymmetric algorithm used to perform
asymmetric encryption on the hash value to obtain the
final digital signature value.
8.5.3.1.2 Benefits
Certificate Authority Service is used to provide certificate service and protocol
management functions, helping users quickly apply for and use certificates.
● Certificate Authority Service provides functions such as configuring and
managing CAs, certificate profiles, and CRLs.
● Certificate Authority Service supports the standard Certificate Management
Protocol (CMP) and privacy CA protocol.
● With Certificate Authority Service, you can quickly apply for and use
certificates, reducing the costs of certificate application.
8.5.3.1.3 Scenario
Based on the functions provided by the Certificate Authority Service, you can
quickly obtain and use certificates by setting CA parameters.

NO TE
The Certificate Authority Service only issues identity certificates for network management
software and devices. It does not issue personal certificates for authentication and signature
for individual users.
NetEco CA Independent Networking
As shown in the figure, in the CA independent networking scenario, after the
Certificate Authority Service is deployed on the NetEco and a CA is created, end
entity certificates can be issued to the NetEco and services or devices. The NetEco
and services or devices can apply for certificates from the NetEco CA through CMP
or privacy CA protocol, and other devices, such as security gateways, can apply for
certificates in offline mode.
This networking applies to small-scale networks where the number of network
devices does not exceed the NetEco CA specifications. It does not apply to
networks where different devices need to be divided into multiple subdomains.
Figure 8-26 NetEco CA Independent Networking
NetEco CA and Operator PKI Co-Networking
As shown in the figure, in the NetEco CA and operator PKI co-networking scenario,
the NetEco subordinate CA issues end entity certificates to the NetEco and services
or devices, and the operator subordinate CA issues end entity certificates to other
devices such as security gateways. The operator PKI root CA certificate needs to be
installed offline for services or devices, and the NetEco root CA certificate needs to
be installed for other devices such as security gateways so that the mutual
authentication can be successful.
This networking applies to the scenario where different subdomains are isolated
from each other.

NetEco CA and Operator PKI Co-Networking, with NetEco CA Serving as
Operator CA's Subordinate CA
As shown in the figure, in the NetEco CA and operator PKI co-networking scenario
where the NetEco CA functions as the operator CA's subordinate CA, the NetEco
subordinate CA issues end entity certificates to the NetEco and services or devices;
and the operator subordinate CA issues end entity certificates to other devices
such as security gateways. The operator PKI root CA certificate needs to be
installed offline for services or devices, and also need to be installed on other
devices such as security gateways so that the mutual authentication can be
successful.
This networking applies to the scenario where a single root CA is used on the
entire network, the root CA and subordinate CAs are deployed separately, and
subordinate CAs are used to divide subdomains.
Figure 8-28 NetEco CA and Operator PKI Co-Networking, with NetEco CA Serving
as Operator CA's Subordinate CA

As shown in the figure, in the NetEco CA and operator PKI co-networking (multi-
operator) scenario, the NetEco and services or devices can apply for certificates
from multiple CAs, and different CAs can be deployed for different operators to
isolate IPsec tunnels between operators. The NetEco and services or devices can
automatically apply for certificates from the NetEco CA through CMP or privacy
CA protocol. Other devices, such as security gateways, need to apply for
certificates offline. The NetEco CA certificate chain and operator PKI certificate
chain need to be installed on services or devices and security gateways. Services or
devices support automatic application and manual import in offline mode during
the application of end entity certificates through CMP or privacy CA protocol.
Figure 8-29 NetEco CA and Operator PKI Co-Networking (Multi-Operator
Scenario)
8.5.3.1.4 Functions
This section describes the functions of the Certificate Authority Service from
aspects of PKI management, protocol configuration and certificate application.

external systems to ensure key security.
8.5.3.1.5 Availability
Before deploying and operating the Certificate Authority Service, you need to learn
the prerequisites. Based on the prerequisites, you can evaluate whether the
Certificate Authority Service can be used and make necessary preparations, such
as checking whether a license and interworking with other products are required.
License Requirements
The Certificate Authority Service is not license-controlled.
Related Products
The Certificate Authority Service does not require the interaction with peripheral
products.
8.5.3.1.6 Principles
This section describes the working mechanism of the Certificate Authority Service
to help you learn how to apply for certificates.
Figure 8-30 shows the working mechanism of the Certificate Authority Service.

When applying for a certificate using the Certificate Authority Service, you need to
configure the CA information, certificate profile, whitelist, and CRL as required.
Certificate application methods:
● Manual: You can apply for a certificate by entering basic information,
uploading a CSR file.
● Automatic: You can apply for a certificate by configuring CMP or privacy CA
protocol information.
8.5.3.2 Certificate Authority Service Configuration Procedure
Before using the Certificate Authority Service, you need to understand the
configuration process so that you can quickly apply for and use certificates.
Root CA Directly Issuing End Entity Certificates
As shown in the figure, the Certificate Authority Service is enabled on the NetEco
and only one CA is deployed. Functioning as both the root CA and lowest-level CA,
the CA issues end entity certificates to the NetEco and services/devices. As the
lowest-level CA, the root CA can issue only end entity certificates and cannot issue
lower-level CAs.
This method applies to small-scale networks. It does not apply to networks where
different devices need to be divided into multiple subdomains.

The configuration procedure is as follows.

Subordinate CA in a Single PKI System Issuing Certificates
As shown in the figure, after the Certificate Authority Service is enabled on the
NetEco, both the root CA and subordinate CAs are deployed. The root CA can only
issue subordinate CAs offline and cannot issue end entity certificates. The lowest-
level subordinate CA can only issue end entity certificates and cannot issue lower-
level subordinate CAs. Three methods are available:
● Method 1: The root CA issues multi-level subordinate CAs. Only the lowest-
level subordinate CA can issue end entity certificates. The number of
subordinate CA levels depends on NetEco resources and specifications. This
method applies to the scenario where the network is divided into multiple
layers of subdomains.

method 1
● Method 2: The root CA issues multiple subordinate CAs. Only the lowest-level
subordinate CA can issue end entity certificates. The number of subordinate
CAs depends on NetEco resources and specifications. This method applies to
the scenario where the network is divided into multiple subdomains.
Figure 8-33 Subordinate CA in a single PKI system issuing certificates -
method 2
● Method 3: The root CA issues multiple subordinate CAs that are deployed on
different NetEcos. Only the lowest-level subordinate CA can issue end entity
certificates. Services/devices that have certificates issued by different
subordinate CAs must have the root CA certificate installed so that the mutual
authentication can be successful. This method applies to the scenario where

method 3

Subordinate CA in a Multi-PKI System Issuing Certificates
As shown in the figure, different root CAs are deployed on different NetEcos. The
peer root CA certificate needs to be installed on services/devices in different
subdomains so that the mutual authentication can be successful. On NetEco-1,

from root CA-1) is deployed. The two root CAs are independent of each other. The
certificates of root CA-1 and root CA-2 need to be installed on service/device-1
and service/device-2 so that the mutual authentication can be successful.
This method applies to the networking scenario where different subdomains are
completely isolated. More subdomains require more root CAs, complicating
management.
Figure 8-35 Subordinate CA in a multi-PKI system issuing certificates

configure the port status, TLS certificate,
privacy CA protocol information, and EK
trusted certificate, and use the privacy
CA request URI to apply for a certificate.
For details, see 8.5.3.10 Global
Configuration and 8.5.3.9 Configuring
the Privacy CA Protocol.
8.5.3.3 Configuring a Certificate Profile
When creating a CA and applying for certificates, you can configure a certificate
profile to avoid repeated configurations and meet certificate application and
usage requirements in different scenarios.
Prerequisites
You have the permissions to add, modify, copy, view, and delete certificate profiles,
and add, modify, view, and delete customized extension items.

● A certificate profile is a set of rules and settings used for certificate
application and management. These rules and settings can be simple or
complex to meet your varying requirements.
● Use a certificate profile whose key algorithm is RSA (3072-bit or longer key)
or ECDSA (256-bit or longer key) during upgrade.
● Predefined profiles are default profiles provided by the system and cannot be
deleted or modified. For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-83. The
following table describes some parameters in the preset profile. The actual
configuration varies according to the information on the page.
Table 8-83 Predefined profile parameters

The default value of Validity offset for a preset certificate profile is -10 seconds,
indicating that the actual validity period of the certificate will be offset forward by 10
seconds when the preset certificate profile is used to apply for a certificate.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose PKI Management > Certificate Profile from the navigation tree on the
left.
Step 3 Click New and set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-84.

Parameter Description Value
Validity After the certificate is applied The value is a
offset(second) for, the time difference between negative integer
the CA and the end entity may ranging from -600 to
exist. However, the time 0, in seconds.
difference may cause the
Default value: -10
certificate to not take effect,
Recommended value:
causing the certificate loading
-10
failure.
For example, if the current time
of the end entity is 12:10:15
and the current time of the CA
service is 12:10:20, and the
certificate effective date is set
to 12:10:20 (CA service time),
the certificate has not taken
effect on the end entity after
being applied for, and the end
entity may fail to load the
certificate. The validity offset is
used to solve this problem. In
the preceding scenario, the
validity offset can be set to -10
seconds. When the CA issues a
certificate, the certificate
effective date is offset to -10
seconds (that is 12:10:15) from
the current server time. In this
way, after the certificate is
issued, the certificate takes
effect on the end entity.

used when a certificate profile
Recommended value:
is used to apply for a certificate.

RSA with a key shorter than 2048
bits and ECDSA with a key shorter
than 224 bits are insecure key
algorithms. RSA that uses a 2048-
bit key size is a medium-strength
algorithm. To ensure system
communication security, you are
advised to use ECDSA with a 256-
bit or longer key or RSA with a
3072-bit or longer key to generate
the private key of the certificate.

NO TE
● Critical: The certificate user performs strong verification on the extensions that are
marked as critical.
● Required: The extension value cannot be empty during certificate issuing.
● Permitted in request: The extension value in the request can be used during certificate
issuing.
If both Required and Permitted in request are selected, the extension value in the request
is used preferentially. If the request does not contain the extension value, the default value
in the certificate template is used. If only Permitted in request is selected, the extension
value in the request is used. If the request does not contain the extension value, the issued

NO TE
You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.

Related Tasks
● Viewing a certificate profile
On the Certificate Profile page, click the name of a certificate profile. Then
you can view the detailed information about this certificate profile.
● Modifying a certificate profile
On the Certificate Profile page, click Modify in the Operation column of a
certificate profile. Then you can modify the configuration of this certificate
profile.
NO TE
● The certificate profile name cannot be changed when you modify the certification
profile configuration.
● The preset certificate profile cannot be modified.
● If the certificate level of a profile is Subordinate CA or End-entity and the profile
has been associated with a CA, the certificate level of the profile cannot be
changed to Root CA.
● Copying a certificate profile
On the Certificate Profile page, click Copy in the Operation column of a
certificate profile. In the displayed Copy Certificate Profile dialog box, enter
the Profile name and click Submit. Then you can copy this certificate profile
and rename it.
● Deleting a certificate profile
On the Certificate Profile page, click Delete in the Operation column of a
certificate profile.
NO TE
The preset certificate profile cannot be deleted.
● Searching for a certificate profile
On the Certificate Profile page, enter a certificate profile name in the search
box and click . The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by
certificate profile name.
● Customizing an extension
On the Custom Extension page, click New. Set Name, OID, and Description
as required, and click Submit. The system displays a message indicating that
adding succeeded. Click OK. You can then use the added custom extension
when creating a certificate profile.

NO TE
● Name: name of the custom extension. Enter a string of 1 to 45 characters. Only
digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-) are
allowed. The value cannot be null or all (case insensitive).
● OID: object identifier. If the custom extension is used in a certificate profile, the
OID is used as the unique identifier of the extension. The OID must contain 3 to
256 characters prefixed with 0., 1., or 2.. If the period (.) is followed by 0, 0 cannot
be followed by other digits. For example, 2.5.29.32.0 is in correct format, but 2.02
is in incorrect format.
● Description: Enter a string of 0 to 128 characters, including digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, spaces, and punctuation marks (,.!:;?)(The punctuation
marks do not distinguish between Chinese and English, but the value cannot end
with ;).
● A maximum of 16 custom extensions can be added.
● If a custom extension has been associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
only the description but cannot delete the extension.
● If a custom extension is not associated with a certificate profile, you can modify
the OID and description and delete the extension.
8.5.3.4 Customizing a CA
If you do not have a CA or have a CA that does not meet the requirements for
issuing certificates, you can configure one on the CA management page to
facilitate your subsequent certificate application and use.
Prerequisites
● Key generation mode can choose to generate CA certificate keys using
Software or HSM. If you select HSM, you need to add the HSM to the system
first. For details about the configuration method, see 8.5.3.11 Managing
HSMs.
● To publish the CRL corresponding to the CA to the CRL server, you need to
add the CRL server information to the system first. For details about the
configuration method, see 8.5.3.6 Configuring a CRL Server.
● You have the permission to add, modify, update, activate, view, and delete
CAs, import and delete cross certificates, download CSRs, and download
associated certificates.
Context
The Certificate Authority Service provides a certificate issuance solution to address
the problems arising from the unavailability of a CA or an existing CA that does
not meet the requirements. When you use the Certificate Authority Service for the
first time or no CA is configured in the current Certificate Authority Service, you
need to create a CA on the configuration wizard page.
NO TICE
When importing an external certificate, ensure that the certificate is issued by a
trusted CA. Otherwise, security risks exist.

Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose PKI Management > CA from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 Click New and set required parameters. For detailed parameter descriptions, see
Table 8-86.
NO TE
It is recommended that the common name of the CA certificate in the certificate chain be
unique.

Step 4 Click Next.
Step 5 In the profile association list, select the profile to be associated.
NO TE
● Configure an associated profile for the CA to issue certificates. The associated profile
must be a sub-CA profile or an end-entity profile.
● A CA must be associated with at least one profile, and a maximum of 16 profiles can be
associated.
Step 6 In the default profile list, select an associated profile as the default profile.

NO TE
● During certificate application using CMP or privacy CA protocol, if the request carries
the profile name parameter, the specified profile is used; if the request does not carry
the profile name parameter, the default profile of the CA is used.
● Only one default profile can be set for a CA.
Step 7 Click Next.
NO TE
● To issue a response protection identity certificate in the next step, select at least one
profile whose certificate level is End entity from the associate profile list.
● If Certificate configuration mode is set to New CSR file, click Submit.
Step 8 Set Issue response protection identity certificate and Set CA certificate to TLS
trust certificate, and click Submit.
Step 9 (Optional) If Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate is set to Yes in Step 8,
you need to determine whether to restart the service immediately.
● In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Now, and then click OK to make the
configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS trust certificate take effect.
● In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Later. You can restart the service on
the Global Configuration > Service Management page.
NO TE
● The service is unavailable during the restart. Wait for several minutes and perform other
operations after the restart is complete.
● On the Global Configuration > Service Management page, you can view the service
status. If the service status changes from Restarting to Running, the service is
restarted.
● After the operation is complete, choose Global Configuration > TLS Configuration to
view the TLS trust certificate corresponding to the current CA. For details, see 8.5.3.10.2
Configuring TLS.
● If the service is not restarted, the configuration of Set CA certificate to TLS trust
certificate does not take effect.
----End
Follow-up Procedure
● Modifying a CA
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click Modify in the Operation column of a
CA to modify its configuration.
NO TE
● When modifying the CA configuration, you can only modify the Max validity
period, Certificate SN length, CRL generation interval, CRL generation time,
CRL overlap time, Include revocation reasons, CRL server, Publication mode,
CRL distribution point, associated profile, and default profile of the CA.
● If the CA status is pending, the CA configuration information cannot be modified.
● Deactivating a CA
For a created and activated CA, you can access the PKI Management > CA
page and click Deactivate in the Operation column of the CA to deactivate

For a created but inactivated CA, you can access the PKI Management > CA
page and click Active in the Operation column of the CA to activate it.
● Downloading a CA certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click Download CA Certificate in the
Operation column of a CA, select and download the CA certificate in the
required file format.
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name. On the page that is
displayed, click Download CA Certificate to download the CA certificate
in .cer format to the local PC.
NO TE
If the CA status is pending, the CA certificate cannot be downloaded.
● Downloading CSR
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click More > Download CSR in the
Operation column of a CA to download the CSR file of the CA.
● Uploading a CA certificate
For a created but pending CA, you can access the PKI Management > CA
page and click More > Upload CA Certificate in the Operation column of
the CA to upload a CA certificate.
NO TE
● The certificate file or certificate chain must be in .cer, .crt, or .pem format. Only
one certificate can be uploaded, and the size of a single file cannot exceed 10 KB.
The certificate file name is a string of 1 to 256 characters containing Chinese
characters, digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens
(-), spaces, dots (.) and round brackets. It cannot start with a dots (.) or space.
A maximum of 10 certificate chain files can be uploaded in the cer, crt, or pem
format with a size smaller than 100 KB. The total size of the uploaded files cannot
exceed 100 KB.
● If the certificate issued by the current CA is used to sign the CMP request packet,
the CA uses the current CA and CA certificate chain to verify the signature
certificate of the request packet.
● For a CA that has been created and is in the pending state, if the CA certificate is
not uploaded within 15 days, the CA will be deleted.
● If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 1024-bit
key size, the certificate file has security risks. You are advised to upload a secure
certificate file. If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA
with a 2048-bit key size, the certificate file is of medium security. You need to
confirm the operation in the Information dialog box. You are advised to upload a
secure certificate file.
● Updating a CA
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click More > Update in the Operation
column of a CA to update the CA. If the CA is the root CA, three certificates
OldWithOld, OldWithNew, and NewWithOld are automatically created after
the CA is updated. If the CA is a sub-CA, an OldWithOld certificate is
automatically created after the CA is updated. You can click the CA name and
download the certificate on the Associate Certificate tab page.
Update a CA created in New CSR file mode: Choose PKI Management > CA

If the CA has issued a response protection certificate or TLS certificate, you
need to update the certificate.
NO TE
● A CA can be updated for a maximum of 16 times.
● When a subordinate CA certificates (created in the New CSR file/Upload
certificate file mode) is updated, the root CA issued by a third-party organization
can be updated only once.
● Cancel the CA update
The update for subordinate CA certificates (created in the New CSR file/
Upload certificate file mode) can be canceled automatically or manually.
– Automatically cancel the update: Choose PKI Management > CA and
click More > Update on the right of a CA. If no new CA certificate is
uploaded within 15 days, the CA status automatically changes from
Updating to Active. In this case, the data generated during the update
becomes invalid and the CA recovers.
– Manually cancel the update: Choose PKI Management > CA and click
More > Cancel Update on the right of a CA. In the displayed Warning
dialog box, click OK. The CA status changes from Updating to Active. In
this case, the data generated during the update becomes invalid and the
CA recovers.
● Deleting a CA
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click More > Delete in the Operation
column of a CA to delete the CA and all configurations related to the CA.
NO TE
The CA can be deleted only when it has not issued a sub-CA or certificate.
Related Tasks
● Querying a CA
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name to view the CA details on
the CA Information tab page, including the status, Certificate SN length,
signature algorithm, associated profile, and default profile.
● Querying a CA certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name to view the CA details on
the CA Certificate tab page, including the version, SN, subject, and validity
period.
NO TE
If the CA status is pending, you cannot view the CA certificate information.
● Querying an associate certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name to view the CA details on
the Associate Certificate tab page, including the version, SN, subject, and
validity period.

NO TE
● OldWithNew: Certificate generated when the CA key is updated. The certificate
contains the public key of the old key. The validity period of the certificate is the
same as that of the old CA. For the entity that uses the new certificate of the root
CA as the trust root, if such an entity is not verified, you need to obtain the
OldWithNew certificate of the root CA. The verification process is to use the root
CA's new key to verify the old key, and then use the old key to verify the end
entities that are originally subordinated to the old root CA.
● NewWithOld: Certificate generated when the CA key is updated, including the
public key of the new key. The effective date of the certificate is that of the new
CA, and the expiration date is that of the old CA. For entities that use the old
certificate of the root CA as the trust root, apply for a new certificate as soon as
possible. During this period, if entities that use the new certificate of the root CA as
the trust root need to be verified, obtain the NewWithOld certificate of the root
CA first. The verification process is to use the root CA's old key to verify the new
key, and then use the new key to verify the end entities that are originally
subordinated to the new root CA.
● Downloading an associate certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name, and click Download on the
Associate Certificate tab page to select and download the CA certificate in
the required file format. If you select to download multiple certificates, the
selected certificates are combined and downloaded to a .pem certificate file.
● Importing a cross-certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. Click a CA name, and click Import
Certificate on the Associated Certificate tab page to import certificates of
other CAs for cross authentication.
NO TE
● The cross-certificate file to be uploaded must be in .cer, .crt, or .pem format. A
maximum of one cross-certificate file can be uploaded at a time. The size of the
file to be uploaded at a time is less than 10 KB. A maximum of 16 cross-certificates
can be uploaded.
● If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 1024-bit
key size, the certificate file has security risks. You are advised to upload a secure
certificate file. If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA
with a 2048-bit key size, the certificate file is of medium security. You need to
confirm the operation in the Information dialog box. You are advised to upload a
secure certificate file.
● Deleting a cross-certificate
Choose PKI Management > CA. On the Associated Certificate tab page,
locate the cross certificate to be deleted and click Delete to delete it.
● Searching for a CA
Choose PKI Management > CA. Enter a CA name in the search box and click
to find the specified CA and view its details. The Certificate Authority
Service supports fuzzy search by CA name.
● Searching for an associated profile
When creating or modifying a CA on the PKI Management > CA page, enter
the name of an associated profile in the search box on the page for setting
associated profiles, and click to find the specified associated profile. The

When creating or modifying a CA on the PKI Management > CA page, enter
the name of a default profile in the search box on the page for setting default
profiles, and click to find the specified default profile. The Certificate
Authority Service supports fuzzy search by default profile name.
8.5.3.5 Applying for a Certificate
On the Certificate Application page, you can apply for a certificate in different
modes as required.
8.5.3.5.1 Application Method 1: Entering Basic Information
To meet different requirements for certificate application, the Certificate Authority
Service allows you to apply for a certificate by entering basic information.
Prerequisites
● On the Certificate Application page, the end entity certificate application
function is disabled by default and the Apply by Basic Info tab is not
displayed. To enable the end entity certificate application function, choose
Global Configuration > Function Management. In this case, the Apply by
Basic Info tab is displayed on the Certificate Application page.
● You have the permissions to view the application list, export the certificate
list, and apply for, update, export, download, view, revoke, and delete
certificates.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Certificate Application > Certificate Application from the navigation
tree on the left.
Step 3 On the Apply by Basic Info tab page, enter certificate application information.
NO TE
● The subject information in the certificate application request must be different from
that of the associated CA. Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● When applying for a certificate by configuring basic information, you can select only a
profile whose certificate level is End entity.
● The certificate validity period is the intersection of the certificate validity period of the
associated CA, the validity period set in the certificate profile, the maximum validity
period of the certificate that can be issued by the associated CA, and the current validity
period.
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
Step 4 Click Submit.

● Viewing a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click the SN of a certificate to
view the certificate details.
NO TE
If the number of certificates issued by the Certificate Authority Service exceeds 80% of
the maximum number supported by the Certificate Authority Service, all expired
certificates are automatically deleted.
● Updating a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Update in the Operation
column of a certificate to update it. In the displayed dialog box, select New
effective time and New expiration time, and click Update. After the
certificate is updated, you can choose whether to download the certificate to
the local computer.
NO TE
● After the certificate is updated, a certificate is added to the certificate
management list. The SN, effective time, and expiration time of the new certificate
are different from those of the original certificate, but other information remains
the same.
● The certificate obtained by using CMP (with the key pair stored on the client),
privacy CA protocol or uploading the CSR file cannot be updated.
● Searching for a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, enter the subject name of the
certificate in the search box for a search. You can also specify the SN, validity
period, revocation reason, issuer or status of the certificate in Advanced
Search. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by subject
name, SN, validity period or issuer.
On the Certificate Application > Application List page, enter the applicant,
CA name, subject or submitted time of the certificate in the search box for a
search. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by applicant,
CA name, subject or submitted time.
● Revoking a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Revoke in the Operation
column of a certificate to revoke it.
NO TICE
Only certificates whose revocation reason is "Certificate is on hold" can have
their revocation canceled. Certificates revoked for other reasons can no longer
be trusted.
● Canceling certificate revocation
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Undo Revoke in the
Operation column of a certificate to cancel the certificate revocation.

Certificate in the Operation column of a certificate to download it to
the local computer.
NO TE
● When downloading a certificate that is requested by entering basic
information, you need to enter a password. The password is a string of 8 to
32 characters containing at least three of the following: digits, uppercase
letters, lowercase letters, and special characters (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?
@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the aforementioned special characters is permitted,
while other characters are not allowed). In addition, the password cannot
contain two or more of the same characters consecutively.
● Only successfully obtained certificates can be downloaded.
– On the Certificate Application > Application List page, click on the
left, click Download on the right of a certificate, and select and
download the certificate in the desired format as required.
– On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Download in the
Operation column of a certificate, select and download the certificate in
the required file format.
NO TE
● File types include Certificate, Private key, and Certificate and private key.
The corresponding file formats are DER(.cer)/PEM(.pem), PKCS#8(.pem), and
PKCS#12(.p12), respectively.
● The file name contains 1 to 20 case-insensitive characters, including digits,
uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores (_), and hyphens (-), but
cannot be null or all.
● The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters containing at least three of the
following: digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters
(!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the aforementioned special
characters is permitted, while other characters are not allowed). In addition,
the password cannot contain two or more of the same characters
consecutively.
● When downloading files in PKCS#12(.p12) or PKCS#8(.pem) format, you need
to set the file password. Keep the file password secure for future use.
● When you download a certificate in a specified file format, the file format
that you can select depends on the certificate application mode.
● Applying for a certificate based on basic information: You can download
a file in DER, PEM, PKCS#12, or PKCS#8 formats.
● Applying for a certificate by uploading a file: You can download a file in
DER or PEM format.
● Applying for a certificate using the privacy CA protocol: You can
download a file in DER or PEM format.
● Applying for a certificate using CMP: If the key pair is stored on the
client, you can download a file in DER or PEM format. If the key pair is
stored on the CA server, you can download a file in DER, PEM, PKCS#12,
or PKCS#8 format.
● Exporting the certificate list
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, click Export List in the upper
right corner, set Start number, and click Submit to export the certificate list.

NO TE
A maximum of 50,000 data records can be exported at a time.
Example 1: If there are 60,000 certificates in the system, you can export them in two
times. Enter 1 and 50001 separately for Start number.
Example 2: If there are 30,000 certificates in the system, enter 1 for Start number to
export the list of all certificates at a time.
● Exporting certificates
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, select the certificates to be
exported, click Export Certificates in the upper right corner, enter the file
name, and click Submit to export the selected certificates.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be exported once. The exported file is in .zip
format, and the certificate file in the .zip package is in .pem format.
● Deleting a certificate
On the PKI Management > Certificate page, select one or more certificates
whose Expiration Time is the previous day (which must be based on UTC) or
earlier, and click Delete in the upper right corner.
In the Warning dialog box, click Export for Backup. In the Export
Certificates dialog box, enter the file name and click Submit to export the
certificates to be deleted to the local PC. After the system displays a message
indicating that the certificates are exported successfully, click OK.
NO TE
A maximum of 100 certificates can be deleted at a time. Deleted certificates cannot be
restored.
8.5.3.5.2 Application Method 2: Uploading a File
To meet different requirements for certificate application, the Certificate Authority
Service allows you to apply for a certificate by uploading a CSR file.
Prerequisites
● The end entity certificate application function has been enabled Global
Configuration > Function Management.
● The CSR file has been obtained in advance.
● You have the permissions to view the application list, export the certificate
list, and apply for, update, export, download, view, revoke, and delete
certificates.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Certificate Application > Certificate Application from the navigation
tree on the left.

Step 4 Click next to CSR file.
Step 5 Select a local CSR file and click Submit.
NO TE
● The subject information in the CSR file must be different from that of the associated CA.
Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● The file to be uploaded must be in .csr, .txt, or .req format, and the size of a single file
cannot exceed 100 KB. The certificate file name is a string of 1 to 256 characters
containing Chinese characters, digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, underscores
(_), and hyphens (-), spaces, dots (.) and round brackets. It cannot start with a dots (.)
or space.
● If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 1024-bit key
size, the certificate file has security risks. You are advised to upload a secure certificate
file. If the key algorithm of the uploaded certificate public key is RSA with a 2048-bit
key size, the certificate file is of medium security. You need to confirm the operation in
the Information dialog box. You are advised to upload a secure certificate file.
● You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
----End

8.5.3.6 Configuring a CRL Server
If you want to publish the CRL corresponding to a CA to a specified CRL server,
you can configure information about the CRL server on the CRL management
page.
Prerequisites
You have the permission to view CRLs, add, modify, delete, and view CRL servers,
and import CRLs.
Context
The Certificate Authority Service supports manual and automatic CRL publishing.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose PKI Management > CRL from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 On the CRL Server tab page, click New and set parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-87.

Related Tasks
● Viewing a CRL server
On the CRL Server tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click the name
of a CRL server to view detailed information about this CRL server.
NO TE
● If the NMS already exists and the FTP, SFTP, and LDAP server information has been
configured, the CRL server of the Certificate Authority Service can integrate the
FTP, SFTP, and LDAP server information of the NMS. The CRL server name is
BUILDIN_CRL_SERVER. In addition, there can be a maximum of one.
● On the PKI Management > CRL page, click the CRL Server tab to view the
detailed information about BUILDIN_CRL_SERVER. The Certificate Authority
Service does not support the modification or deletion of BUILDIN_CRL_SERVER.
● When creating a CA, you can select BUILDIN_CRL_SERVER(Local). Then, the
Certificate Authority Service will release the CRL file corresponding to the CA to the
NMS.
● On the PKI Management > CRL page, click the CRL Server tab. On the CRL Server
tab page, when adding CRL servers, you can add a maximum of five servers for
each type (LDAP/FTP/SFTP) of server (except the local server type).
● Modifying a CRL server
On the CRL Server tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Modify in
the Operation column of a CRL server to modify the configuration of this CRL
server.
● Deleting a CRL server
On the CRL Server tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Delete in
the Operation column of a CRL server to delete this CRL server.
● Importing a CRL
On the CRL Server tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Import
CRL in the Operation column of a CRL server to upload the CRL of the
trusted certificate chain for checking whether the peer server certificate is
revoked.
NO TE
The CRL file to be uploaded must be in .crl or .pem format and the file size cannot
exceed 2 MB.
● Updating a CRL
On the CRL tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Update in the
Operation column of a CRL to manually update the CRL.
● Manually publishing a CRL
On the CRL tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Publish in the
Operation column of a CRL to manually publish the CRL.
● Automatically publishing a CRL
When configuring a CA on the PKI Management > CA page, you can set an
interval for automatically publishing CRLs. Then the system automatically

On the CRL tab page under PKI Management > CRL, enter a CA name, click
, and view CRL information of the CA that is searched out. The Certificate
Authority Service supports fuzzy search by CA name.
● Downloading a CRL
On the CRL tab page under PKI Management > CRL, click Download in the
Operation column of a CRL, select and download the CRL file in the required
file format.
8.5.3.7 Managing a Whitelist
On the Whitelist page, you can add, import, delete, and query whitelists or expired
certificate whitelists, and configure policies.
8.5.3.7.1 Configuring a Whitelist
You can configure a whitelist to verify the common name in the certificate
request. You can configure an expired certificate whitelist to verify the common
name in the certificate request for a initial end entity certificate.
Prerequisites
● You have the permission to add, modify, import, export, view, and delete
whitelists.
● Enabling whitelist verification
Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on
the left, click the Protocol Configuration tab, and click Modify in the
Operation column of a CA. On the page that is displayed, set Verify whitelist
to Yes.
NO TE
● By default, the whitelist verification function is disabled.
● The whitelist verification function takes effect only when you apply for a certificate
using CMP.
● Enabling expired certificate whitelist verification
Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu. Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on
the left, click the Protocol Configuration tab, and click Modify in the
Operation column of a CA. On the page that is displayed, set Enable expired
certificate whitelist to Yes.

NO TE
● By default, the whitelist verification function is disabled.
● The expired certificate whitelist verification function takes effect only when you
apply for a new certificate using an expired initial end entity certificate through
CMP.
● To enable the function of verifying expired certificate whitelists, HTTP must be
used. Therefore, you need to set the protocol of the corresponding CMP
configuration to HTTP. However, risk exists when the expired certificate whitelist
verification function is enabled after the port protocol is set to HTTP. Please modify
the configuration after using the function.
● After the expired certificate whitelist verification function is enabled, you can apply
for a new certificate using an expired initial end entity certificate through CMP.
This operation is risky. You are advised to update the end entity certificate offline.
Context
Whitelists are used for CMP interaction. CA verifies the Common Name (CN) of
the certificate subject in the request.
● If the whitelist verification function is enabled, a certificate can be successfully
obtained only when the common name is in the whitelist after you submit a
certificate request.
● If the expired certificate whitelist verification function is enabled, an expired
initial end entity certificate can be successfully obtained only when the
common name is in the whitelist after you submit a certificate request.
Scenario 1: Adding a Whitelist
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
2. Choose PKI Management > Whitelist from the navigation tree on the left.
3. On the Whitelist Management tab, select Whitelist from the drop-down list
box.
4. Click New to enter the information about the whitelist to be created. For
details about the parameters, see Table 8-88.
Table 8-88 Parameters of a whitelist
Parameter Description
Common Mandatory. The common name is a string of 1 to 64
name characters containing Chinese characters, uppercase letters,
lowercase letters, digits, spaces, hyphens (-), underscores (_),
colons (:), and periods (.), and cannot contain all spaces.
Description optional. The description is a string of 0 to 128 characters
containing digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, spaces,
and special characters ( , . ? ! : ; )(The punctuation marks do
not distinguish between Chinese and English, but the value
cannot end with ;).

NO TE
You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
5. Click Submit.
Related Tasks
● Modifying a whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Whitelist from the drop-down list, and click Modify in the Operation
column of a whitelist to modify the whitelist information.
● Importing a whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Whitelist from the drop-down list, and click Import to manually
import a whitelist.
NO TE
● You can download the template file, set parameters following the instructions, and
upload the file.
● The file to be uploaded must be in .csv format and the size of a single file cannot
exceed 20 MB. A maximum of 50,000 records can be imported at a time.
● Deleting a whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Whitelist from the drop-down list, select the whitelist to be deleted,
and click Delete. Alternatively, you can click Delete in the Operation column
of the whitelist to delete it.
● Searching for a whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Whitelist from the drop-down list, enter a common name in the search
box, and click to find the specified whitelist and view its details. The
Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by whitelist name.
● Exporting a whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Whitelist from the drop-down list, click Export Whitelist, set Start
number, and click Submit. Then the system displays a message indicating
that the export is successful.
NO TE
A maximum of 10,000 data records can be exported at a time.
Example 1: If there are 20,000 records in the system, you can export the records in two
times: Set Start number to 1 and 10001 respectively.
Example 2: If there are 10,000 records in the system, 10,000 whitelist records can be
exported at a time when Start number is set to 1, and 9,999 whitelist records can be
exported at a time when Start number is set to 2.
Scenario 2: Adding an Expired Certificate Whitelist
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main

3. On the Whitelist Management tab, select expired certificate Whitelist from
the drop-down list box.
4. Click New to enter the information about the expired certificate whitelist to
be created. For details about the parameters, see Table 8-89.
Table 8-89 Parameters of an expired certificate whitelist
Parameter Description
Common Mandatory. The common name is a string of 1 to 64
name characters containing Chinese characters, uppercase letters,
lowercase letters, digits, spaces, hyphens (-), underscores (_),
colons (:), and periods (.), and cannot contain all spaces.
Description optional. The description is a string of 0 to 128 characters
containing digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, spaces,
and special characters ( , . ? ! : ; )(The punctuation marks do
not distinguish between Chinese and English, but the value
cannot end with ;).
NO TE
You can click Reset to clear parameter settings.
5. Click Submit. After the configuration is complete, click Submit. Then the
system displays a message indicating that the expired certificate whitelist is
added successfully. Risk exists after an expired certificate whitelist is added.
Therefore, delete the expired certificate whitelist immediately after using it.
Related Tasks
● Modifying an expired certificate whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Expired Certificate Whitelist from the drop-down list, and click
Modify in the Operation column of an expired certificate whitelist to modify
the expired certificate whitelist information.
● Importing an expired certificate whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Expired Certificate Whitelist from the drop-down list, and click
Import to manually import an expired certificate whitelist.
NO TE
● You can download the template file, set parameters following the instructions, and
upload the file.
● The file to be uploaded must be in .csv format and the size of a single file cannot
exceed 20 MB. A maximum of 50,000 records can be imported at a time.
● Risk exists after the expired certificate whitelist is imported. Therefore, delete the
expired certificate whitelist immediately after using it.
● Deleting an expired certificate whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,

can click Delete in the Operation column of the expired certificate whitelist
to delete it.
● Searching for an expired certificate whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab,
select Expired Certificate Whitelist from the drop-down list, enter a
common name in the search box, and click to find the specified whitelist
and view its details. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by
expired certificate whitelist name.
● Exporting an expired certificate whitelist
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Management tab
page, click Export Whitelist, enter Start number, and click Submit. A
message is displayed, indicating that the export is successful.
NO TE
A maximum of 10,000 data records can be exported at a time.
Example 1: If there are 20,000 records in the system, you can export the records in two
times: Set Start number to 1 and 10001 respectively.
Example 2: If there are 10,000 records in the system, 10,000 expired certificate
whitelist records can be exported at a time when Start number is set to 1, and 9,999
expired certificate whitelist records can be exported at a time when Start number is
set to 2.
8.5.3.7.2 Configuring a Whitelist Policy
You can configure a whitelist policy or an expired certificate whitelist policy to
verify the whitelist.
Prerequisites
You have the permission to query and modify whitelist policies.
Context
● If the whitelist verification function is enabled, a certificate can be successfully
obtained only when the common name is in the whitelist or the common
name without the suffix is in the whitelist after you submit a certificate
request.
● If the expired certificate whitelist verification function is enabled, an expired
initial end entity certificate can be successfully obtained only when the
common name is in the whitelist or the common name without the suffix is in
the whitelist after you submit a certificate request.
Scenario 1: Modifying a Whitelist Policy
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
2. Choose PKI Management > Whitelist from the navigation tree on the left.
3. On the Whitelist Policy tab, select Whitelist from the drop-down list box,

whitelist policy is configured successfully. Click OK.
NO TE
● The Common name additional info must be a string of 1 to 32 characters,
including digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters +-./?:().
● You can add a maximum of six common name suffixes.
Related Tasks
Modifying a expired certificate whitelist policy
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Policy tab page, select
Whitelist from the drop-down list, click Modify corresponding to the desired
whitelist and modify it.
Scenario 2: Modifying an Expired Certificate Whitelist Policy
1. Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main
menu.
2. Choose PKI Management > Whitelist from the navigation tree on the left.
3. On the Whitelist Policy tab, select Expired Certificate Whitelist from the
drop-down list box, click Modify in the lower right corner, set Common name
additional info as required, and click Submit. The system displays a message
indicating that the expired certificate whitelist policy is configured
successfully. Click OK.
NO TE
● The Common name additional info must be a string of 1 to 32 characters,
including digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters +-./?:().
● You can add a maximum of six common name suffixes.
Related Tasks
Modifying a expired certificate whitelist policy
Choose PKI Management > Whitelist. On the Whitelist Policy tab, select
Expired Certificate Whitelist from the drop-down list, click Modify corresponding
to the desired whitelist and modify it.
8.5.3.8 Configuring CMP
When applying for a certificate using CMP, you need to configure CMP
information, request verification, and response protection for data encryption.
8.5.3.8.1 Configuring CMP Information
You can configure the allowed signature algorithm for CMP request packets. If the
signature algorithm for CMP request packets sent by the client is not within the
selected signature algorithm, the request is rejected.
Prerequisites

on the Certificate Authority Service > Global Configuration > Port
Management page.
● In the multi-tenant deployment scenarios, the system administrator needs to
enable the port on the Certificate Authority Service > Global Configuration
> Port Management page.
● You have the permission to modify and view CMP configurations.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP from the navigation tree on the left.
Step 3 On the Protocol Configuration tab page, click Modify corresponding to a CA. On
the page that is displayed, set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-90.

Follow-up Procedure
Applying for a certificate based on the CMP request URI
On the Protocol Configuration > CMP page, click the Protocol Configuration
tab. On this tab page, click on the left of a CA name, and copy the CMP
request URI corresponding to the CA for use. The CMP request URIs include the
following types:
● HTTP
– http://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name
– http://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name?certprofile=Certificate
profile name
● One-way authentication or Two-way authentication
– https://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name
– https://IP address:Port number/cmp/CA name?certprofile=Certificate

indicates that a CMP request for applying for a certificate is sent to the Certificate
Authority Service through HTTPS.
NO TE
● The certprofile parameter specifies the end entity profile used for issuing certificates.
This parameter is optional. If this parameter is not specified, the default CA profile is
used for certificate application.
● A CA may be associated with multiple profiles. Therefore, the CMP request URI may
have multiple values. Select a value based on the site requirements.
● The subject information in the certificate application request must be different from
that of the associated CA. Otherwise, certificate application fails.
● The port number can be 26801, 26802, or 26803. In the navigation pane, choose Global
Configuration > Port Management. On the Port Management page, you can view or
configure the protocol corresponding to the port number (HTTPS is recommended
because it is more secure than HTTP).
Related Tasks
● Viewing CMP configuration
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Protocol Configuration tab
page, click on the left of a CA name to access the details page, where you
can check the CMP configuration.
● Searching for CMP configuration
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Protocol Configuration tab
page, enter a CA name in the search box, and click to find the specified
CA and view the detailed protocol configuration of the CA. The Certificate
Authority Service supports fuzzy search by CA name.

Step 5 (Optional) If Certificate configuration mode is set to Upload vendor root CA
certificate and Set as TLS trust certificate is set to Yes in Step 3, you need to
determine whether to restart the service immediately.
● In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Now, and then click OK to make the
configuration of setting the vendor root CA certificate as the TLS trust
certificate take effect.
● In the displayed dialog box, click Restart Later. You can restart the service on
the Global Configuration > Service Management page.

NO TE
● The service is unavailable during the restart. Wait for several minutes and perform other
operations after the restart is complete.
● On the Global Configuration > Service Management page, you can view the service
status. If the service status changes from Restarting to Running, the service is
restarted.
● If the service is not restarted, the configuration of setting the vendor root CA certificate
as the TLS trust certificate does not take effect.
----End
Related Tasks
● Viewing request verification information
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Requester Configuration tab
page, click a request verification name. On the page that is displayed, you can
view the detailed information.
● Searching for request verification information
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Requester Configuration tab
page, enter a request verification name in the name search box and click
to find the specified request verification and view the details. The Certificate
Authority Service supports fuzzy search by request verification name.
● Modifying request verification
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Requester Configuration tab
page, click Modify corresponding to the desired request verification. On the
page that is displayed, modify request verification information.
NO TE
The request verification name cannot be changed.
● Deleting request verification
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Requester Configuration tab
page, click Delete corresponding to the desired request verification.
● Downloading a request verification file.
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Requester Configuration tab
page, click Download corresponding to the desired request verification, select
and download the request verification file in the required format.
NO TE
● File types include Certificate, Private key, and Certificate and private key. The
corresponding file formats are DER(.cer)/PEM(.pem), PKCS#8(.pem), and
PKCS#12(.p12), respectively.
● The password is a string of 8 to 32 characters containing at least three of the
following: digits, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and special characters (!"#$
%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ The input of the aforementioned special characters is
permitted, while other characters are not allowed). In addition, the password
cannot contain two or more of the same characters consecutively.
● When downloading files in PKCS#12(.p12) or PKCS#8(.pem) format, you need to
set the file password. Keep the file password secure for future use.
● You can download the request verification certificate only when the signature type

Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Responder Configuration
tab page, click Modify corresponding to the desired response protection. On
the page that is displayed, modify response protection information.
NO TE
The Associated CA cannot be changed.
● Deleting response protection
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Responder Configuration
tab page, click Delete corresponding to the desired response protection.
● Downloading a response protection certificate
Choose Protocol Configuration > CMP. On the Responder Configuration
tab page, click Download corresponding to the desired response protection,
select and download the response protection certificate in the required
format.
8.5.3.9 Configuring the Privacy CA Protocol
Before applying for a certificate using the Privacy CA protocol, you need to
configure the EK trust certificate or pre-shared password in advance.
8.5.3.9.1 Configuring Privacy CA Protocol Information
To apply for a certificate using the privacy CA protocol, you need to first configure
privacy CA protocol information.
Prerequisites
The port used by the privacy CA protocol is enabled. If the port is not enabled:
● In the non-multi-tenant deployment scenarios, you need to enable the port
on the Certificate Authority Service > Global Configuration > Port
Management page.
● In the multi-tenant deployment scenarios, the system administrator needs to
enable the port on the Certificate Authority Service > Global Configuration
> Port Management page.
● You have the permission to modify and view privacy CA protocol

Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol from the navigation tree
on the left.
Step 3 On the Protocol Configuration tab page, click Modify corresponding to a CA. On
the page that is displayed, set required parameters.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-93.
Table 8-93 Privacy CA protocol parameters
Parameter Description Value
CA Name of a CA. The CA name cannot be
changed.
Default value: none
Recommended value: none
Port Port number corresponding to Default value: 26805
the privacy CA protocol.
Recommended value: 26805

Service using the privacy CA
protocol. The privacy CA
protocol request URI has the
following two formats:
● The request URI contains
the name of the CA that

example, https://IP

the IP address used to

is the API version, caname
is the name of the CA that

of the used certificate
profile.

only the name of a CA that

default profile of the CA is

used to connect to the

version and caname is the
name of the CA that issues
the certificate.

Follow-up Procedure
Applying for a certificate based on the privacy CA request URI
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the Protocol
Configuration tab page, click on the left of a CA name, and copy the privacy
CA request protocol URI of the CA for use.
One-way authentication
● https://IP address:26805/pca/v1/CA name
● https://IP address:26805/pca/v1/CA name?certprofile=Certificate profile name
For example, https://IP address:26805/pca/v1/caname?certprofile=profilename
indicates that a privacy CA request for certificate application is sent to the
Certificate Authority Service through the one-way TLS authentication protocol.
NO TE
● The certprofile parameter specifies the end entity profile used for issuing certificates.
This parameter is optional. If this parameter is not specified, the default CA profile is
used for certificate application.
When you apply for an EK certificate using the privacy CA protocol, the value of Key
usage in the certificate profile must contain Key encipherment but not Digital
signature.
When you apply for an AK certificate using the privacy CA protocol, the value of Key
usage in the certificate profile must contain Digital signature but not Key
encipherment.
● A CA may be associated with multiple profiles. Therefore, the privacy CA protocol
request URI may have multiple values. Select a value based on the site requirements.
● The subject information in the certificate application request must be different from
that of the associated CA. Otherwise, certificate application fails.
Related Tasks
● Viewing privacy CA protocol configuration
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the Protocol
Configuration tab page, click on the left of a CA name to view the
privacy CA protocol details.
● Searching for privacy CA protocol configuration
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the Protocol
Configuration tab page, enter a CA name in the search box, and click to
find the specified CA and view the detailed protocol configuration of the CA.
The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by CA name.
8.5.3.9.2 Configuring the EK Trust Certificate

You have the permission to add, delete, and view EK trust certificates.
Context
If the EK certificate is preinstalled on the device, a user uses the RA client to send
a challenge value request to the Certificate Authority Service using the privacy CA
protocol and import the EK trust certificate chain. The request contains the public
AK and EK certificate. After receiving the request, the Certificate Authority Service
verifies the public AK information and uses the imported EK trust certificate chain
to verify the EK certificate. If the verification is successful, the Certificate Authority
Service returns the encrypted challenge value. The user then uses the RA client to
send a certificate application request to the Certificate Authority Service using the
privacy CA protocol. The request contains the CA name, certificate profile name,
subject information, and challenge value. After receiving the request, the
Certificate Authority Service checks whether the challenge value has expired and
verifies the public AK information. If the verification is successful, the Certificate
Authority Service issues the AK certificate.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol from the navigation tree
on the left.
Step 3 On the EK Trust Certificate Configuration tab page, click New and set the
parameters required for the EK trust certificate.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-94.
Table 8-94 EK trust certificate parameters
Parameter Description Value
Name You can customize the name Enter 1 to 45 characters,
of the EK trust certificate to digits, uppercase letters,
distinguish different certificate lowercase letters, underscores

Recommended value: none

Step 4 After the configuration is complete, click Submit. On the EK Trust Certificate
Configuration tab page, you can view the configured EK trust certificate file.
----End
Related Tasks
● Viewing an EK trust certificate
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the EK Trust
Certificate Configuration tab page, click on the left of an EK trust
certificate name to view its details.
● Searching for an EK trust certificate
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the EK Trust
Certificate Configuration tab page, enter the name of an EK trust certificate
in the search box, and click to find the specified certificate and view its
details. The Certificate Authority Service supports fuzzy search by the name of
the EK trust certificate.
● Deleting an EK trust certificate
Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol. On the EK Trust
Certificate Configuration tab page, click Delete on the right of an EK trust
certificate to delete it.
8.5.3.9.3 Configuring a Pre-Shared Password

You have the permission to add, modify, delete, and view pre-shared passwords.
Context
If the EK certificate is not preinstalled on the device before delivery, the user uses
the privacy CA protocol to send a challenge value request to the Certificate
Authority Service through the remote attestation client, and sets a pre-shared
password on the Certificate Authority Service and remote attestation client for
message authentication. A user logs in to the remote attestation client, and uses
the privacy CA protocol to send a certificate application request to the Certificate
Authority Service. The request contains information, such as the CA name,
certificate profile name, subject information, and challenge value. After obtaining
the request, the Certificate Authority Service checks whether the challenge value
has expired and verifies the AK public key information. If the verification is
successful, the Certificate Authority Service issues the EK certificate.
A user uses the RA client to send a challenge value request to the Certificate
Authority Service using the privacy CA protocol and import the EK trust certificate
chain. The request contains the public AK and EK certificate. After receiving the
request, the Certificate Authority Service verifies the public AK information and
uses the imported EK trust certificate chain to verify the EK certificate. If the
verification is successful, the Certificate Authority Service returns the encrypted
challenge value. The user then uses the RA client to send a certificate application
request to the Certificate Authority Service using the privacy CA protocol. The
request contains the CA name, certificate profile name, subject information, and
challenge value. After receiving the request, the Certificate Authority Service
checks whether the challenge value has expired and verifies the public AK
information. If the verification is successful, the Certificate Authority Service issues
the AK certificate.
Procedure
Step 1 Choose System > About > Certificate Authority Service from the main menu.
Step 2 Choose Protocol Configuration > Privacy CA Protocol from the navigation tree
on the left.
Step 3 On the Pre-Shared Password Configuration tab page, click New and set the
parameters required for the pre-shared password.
NO TE
A maximum of 16 pre-shared passwords can be configured.
For detailed parameter descriptions, see Table 8-95.

Parameter Description Value
Name You can customize the name Enter 1 to 45 characters,
of a pre-shared password to digits, uppercase letters,
distinguish different lowercase letters, underscores
passwords. (_), and hyphens (-) are
allowed. The value cannot be
null or all (case-insensitive).
Default value: none
Recommended value: none

● Recommended value: none
